title: LEGAL-BERT: The Muppets straight out of Law School
why: The foundational legal-domain BERT (pretrained on legislation, cases and contracts); ContractNLI tested it as a backbone (helped evidence, hurt NLI), and it underpins the legal-NLP cluster (LexGLUE, CUAD, LegalBench).

title: HoVer: A Dataset for Many-Hop Fact Extraction And Claim Verification
why: Many-hop fact extraction and claim verification over Wikipedia; the claim-verification lineage (with FEVER) that ContractNLI frames document-level NLI against, and a bridge to the multi-hop QA cluster (HotpotQA).

title: CLAUDETTE: an automated detector of potentially unfair clauses in online terms of service
why: Detects potentially unfair clauses in online terms-of-service; an early legal-clause-classification benchmark with the same flag-the-clause shape as ContractNLI, over a different document type.

title: A Dataset for Statutory Reasoning in Tax Law Entailment and Question Answering
why: Statutory reasoning as entailment + QA over US tax law; the case/statute-law counterpart to ContractNLI's contract NLI, contrasting one-to-one statute hypotheses with many-to-many contract hypotheses.

title: A Benchmark for Lease Contract Review
why: Leivaditi et al.'s lease-agreement clause-extraction benchmark; the direct prior-work contract-IE dataset ContractNLI positions its NLI framing against (alongside CUAD).

title: CoQA: A Conversational Question Answering Challenge
why: Conversational QA over text where each question depends on dialogue history; an NQ reference probing a multi-turn QA axis the span benchmarks miss.

title: A BERT Baseline for the Natural Questions
why: The BERT model NQ points to 'for more recent advances' — the baseline that began closing NQ's headline human-machine gap; completes the NQ difficulty-arc story.

title: The Secret Sharer: Evaluating and Testing Unintended Memorization in Neural Networks
why: The foundational method for measuring unintended memorization of training data (the 'exposure' metric); the privacy-leakage threat that motivates Pile of Law's filtering and links to the memorization/extraction safety line.

title: Documenting Large Webtext Corpora: A Case Study on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus
why: The canonical audit of C4 — what blocklist filtering removes (disproportionately minority-dialect and marginalized-group text) and what contamination it leaves in; the responsible-data-documentation counterpart to Pile of Law's filtering thesis.

title: CCNet: Extracting High Quality Monolingual Datasets from Web Crawl Data
authors: Wenzek, Lachaux, Conneau, Chaudhary, …, Joulin, Grave
why: The web-crawl filtering/dedup pipeline behind many LLM corpora and a row in Pile of Law's Table 1 of pretraining-filter practices; concretizes the ad-hoc filtering baseline the paper critiques.

title: Bias in Bios: A Case Study of Semantic Representation Bias in a High-Stakes Setting
why: The occupation-biography dataset (and gender-bias-in-representations study) that Pile of Law repurposes for its privacy-perturbation and Jane-Doe experiments; a core fairness benchmark in the bias-eval cluster.

title: Deduplicating Training Data Mitigates Privacy Risks in Language Models
why: Shows memorization/extraction risk scales superlinearly with duplication, so dedup sharply cuts privacy leakage; the dedup-as-privacy-defense argument Pile of Law weighs against contextual filtering.

title: RAGTruth: A Hallucination Corpus for Developing Trustworthy Retrieval-Augmented Language Models
why: Word-level hallucination corpus for the generation side of RAG; LegalBench-RAG cites it as the generation-side complement to its retrieval benchmark, extending the RAG-eval line.

title: Evaluation of Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Survey
why: The reference survey of RAG evaluation (targets/datasets/metrics taxonomy); a natural hub node tying together the RAG-eval cluster LegalBench-RAG sits in.

title: Question Answering for Privacy Policies: Combining Computational and Legal Perspectives
why: PrivacyQA, the fourth source dataset behind LegalBench-RAG (with CUAD, ContractNLI, MAUD); completes the legal-NLP source quartet already in the graph.

title: Multi-LexSum: Real-World Summaries of Civil Rights Lawsuits at Multiple Granularities
why: Expert multi-granularity summaries of civil-rights litigation; cited as prior legal-NLP dataset work, broadening the legal cluster beyond contracts.

title: LRAGE: Legal Retrieval Augmented Generation Evaluation Tool
why: A holistic legal-RAG evaluation harness building directly on LegalBench-RAG; a forward citation extending the legal-retrieval-eval line into a reusable framework.

title: SCROLLS: Standardized CompaRison Over Long Language Sequences
why: The standardized long-document benchmark MAUD cites to motivate long-context modeling; directly relevant since 50% of MAUD's deal-point texts exceed 512 tokens.

title: CJRC: A Reliable Human-Annotated Benchmark DataSet for Chinese Judicial Reading Comprehension
why: A large human-annotated Chinese judicial reading-comprehension dataset MAUD cites as the closest cross-lingual analog; extends the legal-RC cluster beyond English.

title: Lawma: The Power of Specialization for Legal Annotation
why: Fine-tuned legal-annotation specialists evaluated on MAUD and related tasks; a forward citation testing whether small specialized models beat frontier LLMs on legal labeling.

title: CHANCERY: Evaluating Corporate Governance Reasoning Capabilities in Language Models
why: A corporate-governance legal-reasoning benchmark citing MAUD; the modern-LLM, M&A-adjacent successor probing reasoning over Delaware governance documents.

title: Computational Law: Datasets, Benchmarks, and Ontologies
why: A survey mapping legal datasets, benchmarks, and ontologies that cites MAUD; useful for cross-linking the legal-eval cluster.

title: Man is to Computer Programmer as Woman is to Homemaker? Debiasing Word Embeddings
why: Quantifies and attempts to remove gender bias in word embeddings; an early mitigation counterpart to the measurement Gender Shades pioneered.

title: Actionable Auditing: Investigating the Impact of Publicly Naming Biased Performance Results of Commercial AI Products
why: The direct Gender Shades follow-up: re-audits the same vendors a year after public naming and documents that the disparities shrank - the empirical test of whether audits change behavior.

title: Face Recognition Vendor Test (FRVT): Performance of Automated Gender Classification Algorithms
why: The NIST gender-classification evaluation precedent Gender Shades explicitly follows; grounds the audit's TPR/FPR-by-subgroup methodology in a government benchmark.

title: SaulLM-7B: A pioneering Large Language Model for Law
why: The first open LLM purpose-built for law (continued-pretrained on a 30B-token legal corpus); the domain-specialization baseline ContractEval argues open models still need to be competitive on legal tasks.

title: LawBench: Benchmarking Legal Knowledge of Large Language Models
why: Benchmarks LLMs across 20 legal tasks spanning memorization, understanding, and application — the broad legal-knowledge eval that complements ContractEval's narrow clause-extraction task.

title: A Reasoning-Focused Legal Retrieval Benchmark
why: Stanford RegLab benchmark for reasoning-heavy legal retrieval; ties the legal-eval cluster directly to the LegalBench / Pile-of-Law authors already in the graph (Guha, Manning, Henderson, Ho).

title: CAIL2018: A Large-Scale Legal Dataset for Judgment Prediction
why: Large-scale (2.6M-case) Chinese legal-judgment-prediction dataset; a foundational pre-LLM legal-NLP benchmark ContractEval cites as the prior generation of legal datasets.

title: AGB-DE: A Corpus for the Automated Legal Assessment of Clauses in German Consumer Contracts
why: Annotated corpus for detecting void clauses in German consumer contracts; a non-English, regulation-specific counterpart to CUAD's clause-review task.

title: SelfCheckGPT: Zero-Resource Black-Box Hallucination Detection for Generative Large Language Models
why: FActScore's no-knowledge-source baseline estimator — the canonical zero-resource hallucination check via sampled-response consistency.

title: Large Language Models Struggle to Learn Long-Tail Knowledge
why: Ties QA accuracy to pretraining-corpus entity frequency — the mechanism behind FActScore's finding that factual precision collapses on rare entities.

title: Fact or Fiction: Verifying Scientific Claims
why: SciFact: the fact-verification benchmark lineage FActScore extends from sentence-level human claims to long-form model generations.

title: Evaluating Verifiability in Generative Search Engines
why: Audit of generative search engines' citation reliability — companion to FActScore's PerplexityAI finding that citations don't correlate with factual precision.

title: Chain-of-Verification Reduces Hallucination in Large Language Models
why: A verify-your-own-draft factuality method that adopts FActScore as its long-form metric — shows the metric's downstream use.

title: When FLUE Meets FLANG: Benchmarks and Large Pretrained Language Model for Financial Domain
why: FLUE — the financial-language-understanding benchmark suite (FPB, FiQA, Headline, NER) that BloombergGPT reports its external financial-task numbers on.

title: BioBERT: a pre-trained biomedical language representation model for biomedical text mining
why: The foundational domain-adapted BERT — the encoder-era precedent for domain LLMs that BloombergGPT cites when motivating in-domain training and a domain tokenizer.

title: SciBERT: A Pretrained Language Model for Scientific Text
authors: Iz Beltagy, Kyle Lo, Arman Cohan
why: Domain-specific scientific LM whose finding — a domain vocabulary overlaps only ~42% with general BERT — is exactly the evidence BloombergGPT cites for training its own finance tokenizer.

title: Large Language Models Are State-of-the-Art Evaluators of Translation Quality
why: GEMBA — the LLM-as-judge successor to trained MT metrics like BLEURT; shows GPT prompting rivals/beats learned metrics on WMT, bridging the metric cluster to the LLM-judge cluster.

title: The price of debiasing automatic metrics in natural language evaluation
why: Percy Liang's analysis of the statistical cost of trusting automatic metrics — quantifies how metric error biases system comparison, a rigor lens for the whole eval-metric cluster.

title: Question Answering as an Automatic Evaluation Metric for News Article Summarization
why: An early QA-based evaluation metric — the 'use a model to probe whether the facts survived' idea that later flowers into QAEval/QuestEval and the atomic-fact factuality line (FActScore).

title: SUM-QE: a BERT-based Summary Quality Estimation Model
why: BLEURT's closest methodological sibling — fine-tunes BERT to predict summary quality with the same reference-based regression recipe, a clean contrast for what BLEURT's synthetic pre-training adds.

title: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
why: DQN — the deep Q-learning result PPO names as a leading contender; the origin of deep RL on the Arcade Learning Environment.

title: Sample Efficient Actor-Critic with Experience Replay
why: ACER — the Atari baseline that edged PPO on final performance (28 vs 19 games) while being far more complex.

title: The Lessons of Developing Process Reward Models in Mathematical Reasoning
why: Direct PRM successor (Qwen) — diagnoses why PRM800K-style step labels are noisy/hard to scale, proposes consensus+LLM-judge labeling and a better PRM eval; the practical sequel to Let's Verify.

title: Improve Mathematical Reasoning in Language Models by Automated Process Supervision
why: OmegaPRM — MCTS-based automated process supervision that removes the human step-labeling bottleneck behind PRM800K, the scalable answer to Lightman's main cost.

title: s1: Simple test-time scaling
why: Budget-forcing test-time scaling — the modern descendant of the best-of-N search regime in which Lightman's PRM beats the ORM; reframes 'spend more test-time compute' for reasoning models.

title: WizardMath: Empowering Mathematical Reasoning for Large Language Models via Reinforced Evol-Instruct
why: RLEIF — reward-model-guided RL for math reasoning contemporary with Let's Verify; a widely-used open math-RL baseline.

title: Training Language Models to Self-Correct via Reinforcement Learning
why: SCoRe (DeepMind) — multi-turn RL that teaches a model to fix its own reasoning steps; the policy-side counterpart to verifying steps with a PRM.

title: Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents
why: CoALA — the conceptual framework situating LMs as agents that SWE-agent cites; a high-connectivity backbone for the agents cluster.

title: RepoBench: Benchmarking Repository-Level Code Auto-Completion Systems
why: Repository-level code completion benchmark in the SWE-bench lineage of moving code eval beyond self-contained functions.

title: CrossCodeEval: A Diverse and Multilingual Benchmark for Cross-File Code Completion
why: Cross-file code completion requiring repo-wide context; the edit-scope expansion SWE-agent cites alongside SWE-bench.

title: Self-Taught Optimizer (STOP): Recursively Self-Improving Code Generation
why: Recursive self-improvement via code (Zelikman, the STaR author); a reasoning/agent crossover SWE-agent cites for code-as-action self-improvement.

title: ML-Bench: Evaluating Large Language Models for Code Generation in Repository-Level Machine Learning Tasks
why: Repository-level ML code-generation agent benchmark; sibling to MLAgentBench/MLE-bench that SWE-agent cites for library-aware code tasks.

title: Muppet: Massive Multi-task Representations with Pre-Finetuning
why: Pre-finetuning on ~50 datasets before task finetuning — the multi-task-representation precursor to the instruction-tuning recipe Flan scales; fills a lineage gap before FLAN/T0.

title: What Language Model Architecture and Pretraining Objective Work Best for Zero-Shot Generalization?
why: Systematic study of encoder-decoder vs decoder-only and span-corruption vs causal-LM for zero-shot generalization — the architecture×objective question Flan probes across T5/PaLM/U-PaLM.

title: CrossFit: A Few-shot Learning Challenge for Cross-task Generalization in NLP
why: A 160-task cross-task few-shot generalization benchmark that helped define how to measure the held-out generalization instruction tuning targets.

title: Adapting Language Models for Zero-shot Learning by Meta-tuning on Different NLP Tasks
why: Meta-tuning a model on many NLP tasks reframed as yes/no questions to improve zero-shot — an early instruction-tuning precursor in Flan's reference lineage.

title: Explain Yourself! Leveraging Language Models for Commonsense Reasoning
why: CoS-E: human-written rationales for commonsense QA — foundational rationale/CoT-finetuning data that Flan cites for reasoning-via-finetuning.

title: DeepSeek LLM: Scaling Open-Source Language Models with Longtermism
why: The general-purpose base of the DeepSeek lineage — DeepSeek-Coder's architecture/optimizer follow it, and v1.5 is continued from DeepSeek-LLM-7B; root of DeepSeekMath/V2/V3/R1.

title: DeepSeek-Coder-V2: Breaking the Barrier of Closed-Source Models in Code Intelligence
why: The direct successor (MoE, 338 languages, 128K context) that surpassed GPT-4-Turbo on code; used as a model by Arena-Hard, PlanSearch and R1 in the existing graph.

title: AutoCodeRover: Autonomous Program Improvement
why: Autonomous program-improvement SWE agent; a direct SWE-Bench Lite baseline (19.0) compared against OpenHands' CodeActAgent.

title: OpenAgents: An Open Platform for Language Agents in the Wild
why: The closest sibling open platform for language agents; a direct contrast to OpenHands' generalist-agent framing.

title: Language Agents as Optimizable Graphs
why: GPTSwarm — models multi-agent systems as optimizable graphs; the agent OpenHands ships and uses to lead GAIA (32.1).

title: Autonomous Evaluation and Refinement of Digital Agents
why: Auto Eval & Refine — the strongest WebArena baseline (20.2) in OpenHands' table; GPT-4 + Reflexion with a GPT-4V reward model.

title: Lemur: Harmonizing Natural Language and Code for Language Agents
why: Harmonizes language and code for agents; a WebArena baseline OpenHands measures against and an open code-agent foundation model.

title: TravelPlanner: A Benchmark for Real-World Planning with Language Agents
why: A real-world, constraint-heavy planning benchmark for language agents — the long-horizon planning failure mode RefactorBench surfaces, evaluated in a non-code domain.

title: MASAI: Modular Architecture for Software-engineering AI Agents
why: A modular SWE-agent architecture (Microsoft Research India) competitive on SWE-bench — a design point in the agent-interface lineage RefactorBench studies.

title: CodePlan: Repository-level Coding using LLMs and Planning
why: Frames repository-level code change as a planning problem over a dependency graph — the multi-file, cross-dependency reasoning RefactorBench is built to test.

title: CodexGraph: Bridging Large Language Models and Code Repositories via Code Graph Databases
why: Gives agents a queryable code-graph interface over a repo — a concrete answer to the localization failure (agents not finding the files to edit) RefactorBench isolates.

title: Diversity Empowers Intelligence: Integrating Expertise of Software Engineering Agents
why: A committee of diverse SWE agents (DEI, Salesforce) that topped SWE-bench — the multi-agent scaffolding direction RefactorBench contrasts with its single-agent state-aware fix.

title: Paper2Code: Automating Code Generation from Scientific Papers in Machine Learning
authors: Minju Seo, Jinheon Baek et al.
why: Turns an ML paper into a runnable repository via a multi-agent planning→coding pipeline — the paper→code task PaperBench grades, approached as a system rather than a benchmark.

title: EXP-Bench: Can AI Conduct AI Research Experiments?
authors: Patrick Tser Jern Kon, Jiachen Liu et al.
why: Tasks agents with designing and running end-to-end AI research experiments drawn from real papers — a sibling research-agent benchmark to PaperBench's from-scratch replication.

title: SciReplicate-Bench: Benchmarking LLMs in Agent-driven Algorithmic Reproduction from Research Papers
authors: Yanzheng Xiang, Hanqi Yan et al.
why: Benchmarks agent-driven algorithm reproduction from paper method sections with code + reasoning grading — the closest direct sibling to PaperBench's replication task.

title: AI Sandbagging: Language Models can Strategically Underperform on Evaluations
authors: Teun van der Weij, Felix Hofstätter et al.
why: Shows models can be prompted or fine-tuned to strategically underperform on capability evals — the eval-integrity threat any capability benchmark must guard against (a PaperBench reference).

title: Self-Taught Evaluators
authors: Tianlu Wang, Ilia Kulikov et al.
why: Meta's method for training strong LLM judges from synthetic preference data without human labels — the scalable-judge direction PaperBench's SimpleJudge/JudgeEval motivates (a PaperBench reference).

title: ToolRL: Reward is All Tool Learning Needs
why: Reframes tool use as an RL problem with verifiable rewards over tool calls — the post-training successor to Gorilla's supervised retrieve-aware finetuning, bridging the tool-use and RLVR clusters.

title: APIGen: Automated Pipeline for Generating Verifiable and Diverse Function-Calling Datasets
why: Salesforce's execution-verified function-calling data pipeline — the direct methodological descendant of Gorilla's Self-Instruct-for-APIs, fixing its name-match-only verification with real execution checks.

title: ToolSandbox: A Stateful, Conversational, Interactive Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Tool Use Capabilities
why: Apple's stateful, multi-turn, execution-grounded tool-use benchmark — the modern answer to APIBench's single-call static AST-match metric.

title: ToolACE: Winning the Points of LLM Function Calling
why: A function-calling data-synthesis + verification pipeline whose 8B model rivals GPT-4 on BFCL — the tool-use lineage Gorilla started, taken to accuracy/complexity/diversity-controlled synthetic data.

title: xLAM: A Family of Large Action Models to Empower AI Agent Systems
why: Salesforce's open 'large action model' family purpose-built for function calling/agents — the model-side continuation of Gorilla's API-calling-LLM thesis.

title: CollabLLM: From Passive Responders to Active Collaborators
why: The most direct sibling — trains LLMs to shift from passive responders to active collaborators in multi-turn dialogue; huang-2025 builds directly on its collaborative-partner framing.

title: DOLOMITES: Domain-Specific Long-Form Methodical Tasks
why: The benchmark huang-2025 masks to manufacture ambiguity — 519 expert writing templates across 25 domains with checklist evaluation; the substrate for the whole proactive-gathering setup.

title: Learning to Clarify: Multi-turn Conversations with Action-Based Contrastive Self-Training
why: Prior clarification-as-learning work (action-based contrastive self-training for multi-turn clarification) — a key lineage huang-2025 distinguishes proactive gathering from.

title: SciConvBench: Benchmarking LLMs on Multi-Turn Clarification for Task Formulation in Computational Science
why: 2026 benchmark that directly evaluates the multi-turn clarification skill huang-2025 trains, in a computational-science setting.

title: InfoGatherer: Principled Information Seeking via Evidence Retrieval and Strategic Questioning
why: 2026 successor formalizing strategic information-seeking via evidence retrieval — extends huang-2025's evidence-grounded questioning reward.

title: Mixtral of Experts
why: The open sparse-MoE model OR-Bench uses to generate and rewrite its toxic seeds (chosen for weak safety restrictions + high quality); a foundational MoE LLM report.

title: WildGuard: Open One-Stop Moderation Tools for Safety Risks, Jailbreaks, and Refusals of LLMs
why: Open moderation model/dataset that jointly detects harmful prompts, harmful responses, and refusals — the moderation-tooling sibling to OR-Bench's ensemble moderator.

title: SALAD-Bench: A Hierarchical and Comprehensive Safety Benchmark for Large Language Models
why: A hierarchical, large-scale LLM safety benchmark — the toxic-rejection-axis sibling to OR-Bench, covering the harm side of the safety/over-refusal trade-off.

title: TrustLLM: Trustworthiness in Large Language Models
why: Broad trustworthiness benchmark spanning safety, fairness, and robustness — situates over-refusal within the wider trust landscape and explicitly studies over-cautiousness.

title: MART: Improving LLM Safety with Multi-round Automatic Red-Teaming
why: Multi-round automatic red-teaming for LLM safety, cited in OR-Bench's intro as a core alignment technique — the adversarial-generation counterpart to OR-Bench's benign-prompt generation.

title: A StrongREJECT for Empty Jailbreaks
why: Shows many 'successful' jailbreaks produce useless/low-quality output; a rigorous jailbreak benchmark + autograder that directly critiques HarmBench-style attempt-only ASR.

title: Agent-SafetyBench: Evaluating the Safety of LLM Agents
why: Extends red-teaming from chat to agentic settings (tool use, environments); a safety benchmark for LLM agents, pairing the agent and safety clusters.

title: AutoDAN-Turbo: A Lifelong Agent for Strategy Self-Exploration to Jailbreak LLMs
why: A lifelong agent that self-discovers jailbreak strategies; SOTA automated red-teaming building on AutoDAN, evaluated on HarmBench.

title: Many-shot Jailbreaking
why: Landmark long-context attack: hundreds of in-context faux-dialogue examples override safety training; jailbreak success scales with the context window.

title: Aegis2.0: A Diverse AI Safety Dataset and Risks Taxonomy for Alignment of LLM Guardrails
why: NVIDIA's diverse AI-safety dataset + risk taxonomy for training content-moderation guardrails; a defense-side counterpart to HarmBench's attack catalogue.

title: Pretraining Language Models with Human Preferences
why: The pretrain-from-values approach Jailbroken points to as the way past competing objectives — bake human preferences into pretraining instead of bolting safety on afterward.

title: Automatically Auditing Large Language Models via Discrete Optimization
why: Steinhardt-group automated auditing via discrete prompt optimization — the conceptual precursor to GCG-style adversarial-suffix search that Jailbroken cites.

title: Fundamental Limitations of Alignment in Large Language Models
why: Theoretical companion to Jailbroken's 'jailbreaks are inherent' thesis: any alignment that merely attenuates rather than removes bad behavior can be elicited by a long-enough prompt (behaviour-expectation bound).

title: On the Impossible Safety of Large AI Models
why: Formal impossibility argument that very large models cannot be simultaneously highly accurate and safe — mathematical backing for why safety training keeps failing.

title: On Second Thought, Let's Not Think Step by Step! Bias and Toxicity in Zero-Shot Reasoning
why: The few-shot GPT-4 sampling recipe Jailbroken used to build its 317-prompt held-out harmful dataset; also shows chain-of-thought can amplify bias and toxicity.

title: In-context Learning and Induction Heads
why: Anthropic's mechanistic account of in-context learning via induction heads — the leading circuit-level explanation for the emergent few-shot abilities Sparks marvels at.

title: GPT Takes the Bar Exam
why: Early professional-exam LLM eval (the bar) — the 'GPT passes the X exam' capability genre Sparks generalizes and GPT-4 later headlined.

title: Dissociating language and thought in large language models
why: The influential cognitive-science rebuttal: separates formal linguistic competence from functional reasoning — the sharpest counter to Sparks' understanding/AGI framing.

title: Transformers Learn Shortcuts to Automata
why: Shows transformers solve sequential-reasoning tasks via shallow shortcuts rather than true algorithms — a mechanistic basis for Sparks' planning and arithmetic failure cases.

title: The Text Anonymization Benchmark (TAB): A Dedicated Corpus and Evaluation Framework for Text Anonymization
why: The PII-detection benchmark Sparks used to show GPT-4 (77.4%) beats Microsoft's Presidio (40.8%); a real outgoing edge target for the Sparks node.

title: XCOPA: A Multilingual Dataset for Causal Commonsense Reasoning
why: The multilingual causal-commonsense benchmark MGSM extends to and sets a new SOTA (89.9%) on; a direct outgoing-edge target for the MGSM node.

title: XL-WiC: A Multilingual Benchmark for Evaluating Semantic Contextualization
why: The multilingual word-in-context benchmark MGSM's second extension experiment uses; the one task where chain-of-thought gave no lift.

title: Nemotron-CrossThink: Scaling Self-Learning beyond Math Reasoning
why: The closest all-domain RLVR baseline General-Reasoner compares head-to-head in Table 2 — self-learning verifiable rewards beyond math.

title: MAmmoTH2: Scaling Instructions from the Web
why: The WebInstruct corpus that General-Reasoner's 230K verifiable-QA dataset is mined and filtered from.

title: Qwen2.5-Math Technical Report: Toward Mathematical Expert Model via Self-Improvement
why: The base of the General-Verifier (Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B) and a foundational math-RL model line (~1k citations).

title: Crossing the Reward Bridge: Expanding RL with Verifiable Rewards Across Diverse Domains
why: Concurrent RLVR-across-domains work — the verifiable-reward-beyond-math line General-Reasoner positions itself against.

title: Demystifying Long Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs
why: The long-CoT analysis framing the overthinking/length tradeoffs General-Reasoner contrasts (its ~1000-token traces vs 32k-token DeepScaleR).

title: Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality
why: word2vec — the skip-gram / negative-sampling word embeddings BERT's contextual representations supersede; a foundational pre-LLM hub cited across NLP.

title: GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation
why: GloVe — the count-based global word vectors that, with word2vec, defined the static-embedding era BERT replaced; ~35k citations and a heavily-cited graph upstream.

title: Google's Neural Machine Translation System: Bridging the Gap between Human and Machine Translation
why: Introduced WordPiece subword tokenization — the exact tokenizer BERT adopts — and scaled seq2seq NMT; a direct methodological ancestor.

title: Universal Language Model Fine-tuning for Text Classification
why: ULMFiT — pre-train-an-LM-then-fine-tune for NLP, the transfer-learning recipe BERT generalized alongside ELMo and GPT (S2 arxiv stub undercounts; resolve canonical record on processing).

title: Semi-supervised Sequence Learning
why: The earliest LM-pretraining-then-fine-tuning result for sequence models — the conceptual ancestor of the BERT/GPT transfer paradigm.

title: SpanBERT: Improving Pre-training by Representing and Predicting Spans
why: Same-week sibling to RoBERTa (shared authors) that questioned NSP and improved span-based pretraining; informs RoBERTa's NSP-removal.

title: ELECTRA: Pre-training Text Encoders as Discriminators Rather Than Generators
source: Lit search: BERT pretraining efficiency (via lan-2019-albert)
why: Replaced-token-detection pretraining that matches RoBERTa/XLNet/ALBERT at a fraction of the compute; cites ALBERT and is the efficiency-focused sibling in the BERT-era encoder cluster.

title: DistilBERT, a distilled version of BERT: smaller, faster, cheaper and lighter
why: The distillation counterpart to ALBERT's parameter reduction — 40% smaller, 60% faster BERT retaining 97% of performance; the other major 2019 'shrink BERT' line.

title: TinyBERT: Distilling BERT for Natural Language Understanding
why: Two-stage Transformer distillation (pretraining + task) for compact BERT; rounds out the model-compression cluster alongside DistilBERT and ALBERT.

title: Large Batch Optimization for Deep Learning: Training BERT in 76 minutes
why: Introduces the LAMB optimizer ALBERT trains with (batch 4096); the large-batch optimization that made fast pretraining of BERT-scale encoders practical.

title: Universal Transformers
why: The cross-layer parameter-sharing precedent ALBERT explicitly compares against; ALBERT's weight-sharing finding (embeddings oscillate, not converge) is framed directly against UT/DQE.

title: Pointer Sentinel Mixture Models
authors: Stephen Merity, Caiming Xiong, James Bradbury, Richard Socher
why: Introduces the WikiText-2/WikiText-103 language-modeling benchmarks GPT-2 (and nearly every LM paper) reports perplexity on.

title: One Billion Word Benchmark for Measuring Progress in Statistical Language Modeling
why: The classic 1BW LM benchmark — the lone dataset GPT-2 fails to beat zero-shot, because its sentence-level shuffling destroys long-range structure.

title: Regularizing and Optimizing LSTM Language Models
authors: Stephen Merity, Nitish Shirish Keskar, Richard Socher
why: AWD-LSTM, the pre-Transformer LM SOTA on Penn Treebank/WikiText that GPT-2's zero-shot perplexities surpass.

title: Unsupervised Machine Translation Using Monolingual Corpora Only
why: A foundational unsupervised-MT method; GPT-2's zero-shot translation is benchmarked against this lineage of monolingual-only systems.

title: A Simple Method for Commonsense Reasoning
authors: Trieu H. Trinh, Quoc V. Le
why: The LM-ensemble Winograd Schema baseline GPT-2 improves on by 7% (to 70.70%), pioneering LMs as zero-shot commonsense reasoners.

title: Representation Learning with Contrastive Predictive Coding
authors: Aäron van den Oord et al.
why: Origin of the InfoNCE loss MoCo (and most contrastive SSL) optimizes; the foundational predict-the-future-in-latent-space contrastive objective.

title: Improved Baselines with Momentum Contrastive Learning
why: MoCo v2 — an MLP projection head plus stronger augmentation push ResNet-50 linear accuracy 60.6%->71.1%; the direct successor cited in the explainer.

title: Unsupervised Feature Learning via Non-parametric Instance Discrimination
why: The instance-discrimination pretext task and memory-bank mechanism MoCo builds on and benchmarks its queue against.

title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
why: ResNet — the backbone every MoCo result runs on and one of the most-cited papers in ML (~231k); a major architectural hub linking the vision nodes.

title: Contrastive Multiview Coding
why: A contemporary multiview contrastive method MoCo compares against; extends instance discrimination to agreement across multiple sensory views.

title: Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift
why: The standardize-activations-per-minibatch trick that made very deep nets trainable; SimCLR's Global BN fix is a direct descendant and nearly every CNN/transformer node depends on it — a high-degree foundational hub.

title: FixMatch: Simplifying Semi-Supervised Learning with Consistency and Confidence
why: The dominant consistency-regularization recipe for semi-supervised learning; SimCLR benchmarks its 1%/10%-label results directly against FixMatch (Table 7), the natural label-efficiency counterpoint.

title: SGDR: Stochastic Gradient Descent with Warm Restarts
why: Introduces the cosine learning-rate schedule SimCLR uses by default (cosine decay without restarts); a small but ubiquitous training-schedule primitive across the foundational-methods cluster.

title: FaceNet: A Unified Embedding for Face Recognition and Clustering
authors: Florian Schroff, Dmitry Kalenichenko, James Philbin
why: The triplet-loss / metric-learning ancestor of contrastive embedding; SimCLR compares NT-Xent against its margin-triplet objective (Tables 2/4), and the retrieval-embedding line traces back through it.

title: Accurate, Large Minibatch SGD: Training ImageNet in 1 Hour
why: The linear LR-scaling + warmup playbook for large-batch training that SimCLR and MoCo rely on to train at batch sizes up to 8192 — a prerequisite for the in-batch-negative contrastive recipe.

title: Mixed Precision Training
why: FP16-with-loss-scaling on Tensor Cores — the precision technique Megatron and essentially every large-model trainer relies on to fit and speed up training.

title: GPipe: Efficient Training of Giant Neural Networks using Pipeline Parallelism
why: The pipeline-parallelism framework Megatron calls itself orthogonal and complementary to — the other half of 3D large-model parallelism.

title: ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models
why: Shards optimizer state/gradients/params across data-parallel replicas (DeepSpeed) — the memory-redundancy axis Megatron leaves untouched; pairs with tensor parallelism in every large-model stack.

title: Efficient Large-Scale Language Model Training on GPU Clusters Using Megatron-LM
why: The direct follow-up that composes tensor, pipeline, and data parallelism (PTD-P / 3D parallelism) to scale Megatron to thousands of GPUs and the trillion-parameter regime.

title: Reducing Activation Recomputation in Large Transformer Models
why: Megatron's sequence-parallelism + selective activation recomputation upgrade — cuts the activation-memory growth this paper left unsolved (the 'Megatron-2' systems result).

title: Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces
why: The headline S4 successor: makes the SSM parameters input-dependent (selective) with a hardware-aware parallel scan, matching Transformers on language while scaling linearly in length — the paper that turned the SSM line into a real attention alternative.

title: HiPPO: Recurrent Memory with Optimal Polynomial Projections
authors: Albert Gu, Tri Dao, Stefano Ermon, Atri Rudra, Christopher Ré
why: The continuous-time memorization theory S4 is built on — defines the special A matrices whose initialization S4's own ablation shows is the true source of its accuracy.

title: Combining Recurrent, Convolutional, and Continuous-time Models with Linear State-Space Layers
authors: Albert Gu, Isys Johnson, Karan Goel, Khaled Saab, Tri Dao, Atri Rudra, Christopher Ré
why: S4's immediate predecessor (the LSSL) — unified RNN/CNN/continuous-time views but cost O(N^2 L) and was numerically unstable; S4 is its efficient, stable reparameterization.

title: Transformers are RNNs: Fast Autoregressive Transformers with Linear Attention
authors: Angelos Katharopoulos, Apoorv Vyas, Nikolaos Pappas, François Fleuret
why: Linear-attention Transformer that switches to a stateful recurrent view at inference — an S4 efficiency baseline and a conceptual bridge between attention and recurrence.

title: Generating Long Sequences with Sparse Transformers
authors: Rewon Child, Scott Gray, Alec Radford, Ilya Sutskever
why: Child et al.'s factorized sparse attention for long sequences — an early efficient-attention landmark cited by S4 and the broader long-context literature.

title: Transformer-XL: Attentive Language Models Beyond a Fixed-Length Context
why: The segment-recurrence long-context LM Longformer benchmarks against on enwik8; a foundational efficient-long-sequence Transformer in the same cluster as FlashAttention/S4/LRA.

title: Compressive Transformers for Long-Range Sequence Modelling
why: DeepMind's compressed-memory long-range LM; an enwik8 SOTA baseline Longformer compares to and a node in the long-context cluster.

title: ETC: Encoding Long and Structured Inputs in Transformers
why: The contemporaneous local+global sparse-attention model BigBird extends; Longformer discusses it directly as the closest concurrent work.

title: PEGASUS: Pre-training with Extracted Gap-sentences for Abstractive Summarization
why: The summarization-pretrained model BigBird's summarizer initializes from and that LED's arXiv-summarization results are benchmarked against.

title: GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head Checkpoints
why: The grouped-query attention method Llama 2's 34B/70B adopt to cut KV-cache cost at inference — the architectural delta from Llama 1 and a building block across efficient-inference LLMs.

title: The RefinedWeb Dataset for Falcon LLM: Outperforming Curated Corpora with Web Data, and Web Data Only
why: Falcon is Llama 2's headline open base-model baseline; RefinedWeb is the filtered-web pretraining corpus behind it — a key data-quality reference point.

title: Gemma: Open Models Based on Gemini Research and Technology
why: Google's open-weight family that benchmarks directly against Llama 2 — the other pole of the open base-model landscape and a natural neighbor node.

title: Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer
why: The original sparsely-gated Mixture-of-Experts layer — the architectural ancestor of every modern MoE model (Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3); GPT-4 cites it and the graph's MoE lineage has no root node.

title: Scaling Laws for Autoregressive Generative Modeling
why: Extends Kaplan-style scaling laws across modalities; the methodological basis for GPT-4's predictable-scaling loss prediction.

title: ST-MoE: Designing Stable and Transferable Sparse Expert Models
why: Makes sparse expert models stable and transferable; a bridge between the original MoE layer and MoE frontier models, cited by GPT-4.

title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention
why: vLLM / PagedAttention — the serving system Mistral 7B ships on; foundational LLM-inference infra cited across the open-model cluster.

title: SocialIQA: Commonsense Reasoning about Social Interactions
why: SIQA — social-commonsense benchmark in Mistral's 0-shot reasoning category.

title: GPT-NeoX-20B: An Open-Source Autoregressive Language Model
why: EleutherAI's open 20B LM trained on The Pile — the model that put the corpus to work; anchors the open-LM side of the graph.

title: Dolma: an Open Corpus of Three Trillion Tokens for Language Model Pretraining Research
why: AI2's 3T-token open corpus (OLMo) — The Pile's modern successor in scale and documentation; direct descendant worth contrasting.

title: RedPajama: an Open Dataset for Training Large Language Models
why: Open reproduction-and-extension of the LLaMA/Pile data lineage; a key node in the open-pretraining-data cluster.

title: The FineWeb Datasets: Decanting the Web for the Finest Text Data at Scale
why: State-of-the-art web-data curation that revisits the diversity-vs-filtering question The Pile raised; heavily cited successor corpus.

title: Deduplicating Training Data Makes Language Models Better
why: Directly addresses the train/test-leakage limitation The Pile acknowledges; foundational data-dedup result for pretraining.

title: Agent Q: Advanced Reasoning and Learning for Autonomous AI Agents
why: MCTS-guided search + DPO self-improvement for web agents, evaluated on WebShop/real bookings — a key RL-for-web-agents method citing Mind2Web.

title: Multimodal Web Navigation with Instruction-Finetuned Foundation Models
why: WebGUM: instruction-finetuned vision-language web agent contemporaneous with Mind2Web; the multimodal counterpart to MindAct's text-only approach.

title: WebLINX: Real-World Website Navigation with Multi-Turn Dialogue
why: Real-world conversational web-navigation benchmark (100K interactions) extending the Mind2Web lineage to multi-turn dialogue; strong eval-map fit.

title: AutoDroid: LLM-powered Task Automation in Android
why: LLM-driven Android GUI automation; connects the web-agent cluster to the mobile/GUI grounding nodes (AndroidWorld, OS-Atlas).

title: Agent Workflow Memory
why: Induces reusable workflows from agent trajectories to improve long-horizon web tasks (Mind2Web/WebArena); a memory-method node for the agent cluster.

title: AEGIS: Online Adaptive AI Content Safety Moderation with Ensemble of LLM Experts
why: NVIDIA's adaptive content-safety guardrail with a 13-category taxonomy — a direct descendant/competitor of Llama Guard to anchor the moderation cluster.

title: Llama Guard 3 Vision: Safeguarding Human-AI Image Understanding Conversations
why: The multimodal successor of this very paper — extends the input-output safeguard to image+text, a natural next node off Llama Guard.

title: R-Judge: Benchmarking Safety Risk Awareness for LLM Agents
why: Agent-level safety benchmark that bridges the safety and agents clusters; judges whether agents recognize risk in their own action traces.

title: SafetyBench: Evaluating the Safety of Large Language Models with Multiple Choice Questions
why: Large multiple-choice safety benchmark across 7 harm categories (EN+ZH) — a widely-cited safety eval complementing the moderation-classifier line.

title: Building Guardrails for Large Language Models
why: Survey/framework on LLM guardrails (Llama Guard, NeMo Guardrails, Guardrails AI) — situates the input-output safeguard design in the broader landscape.

title: Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units
why: BPE subword tokenization — the vocabulary scheme GPT-1/GPT-2/GPT-3 (and most LLMs) are built on.

title: Aligning Books and Movies: Towards Story-like Visual Explanations by Watching Movies and Reading Books
why: BooksCorpus — the 7,000-book pre-training corpus behind GPT-1, GPT-2, and BERT.

title: Generating Wikipedia by Summarizing Long Sequences
why: The decoder-only Transformer (T-DMCA) variant GPT-1 explicitly adapts as its language-model architecture.

title: Learned in Translation: Contextualized Word Vectors
why: CoVe — ELMo's direct predecessor: contextualized word vectors from a pretrained MT encoder; ELMo's headline contrast (+4.7 vs CoVe's +1.8 on SQuAD) is against this.

title: Exploring the Limits of Language Modeling
why: The CNN-BIG-LSTM language model that ELMo's biLM is a halved version of; a foundational large-scale LM scaling paper.

title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
why: Ubiquitous optimizer (167k citations) used to train the biLM; cited by nearly every LM/eval node in the graph — a major foundational hub.

title: Semi-supervised sequence tagging with bidirectional language models
why: TagLM — the immediate precursor where Peters et al. first injected pretrained biLM features into a task model (top layer only); ELMo generalizes it to all layers.

title: Language Models Learn to Mislead Humans via RLHF
why: Direct evidence for the obfuscation thesis: RLHF teaches models to convince humans they're right even when wrong (U-Sophistry), the human-facing analogue of hiding intent from a monitor.

title: Prover-Verifier Games improve legibility of LLM outputs
why: OpenAI's legibility training via prover-verifier games — the constructive counterpart to the monitorability tax, and a candidate for productively pressuring CoTs without inducing obfuscation.

title: How to Catch an AI Liar: Lie Detection in Black-Box LLMs by Asking Unrelated Questions
why: Black-box lie detection — a monitoring approach that reads output patterns rather than the CoT, a complement when chains-of-thought become obfuscated.

title: Eliciting Latent Knowledge from Quirky Language Models
why: ELK probes that recover what a model 'knows' independent of what it says — the activation-monitoring fallback the paper invokes for when CoT monitoring fails.

title: Correlated Proxies: A New Definition and Improved Mitigation for Reward Hacking
why: Formalizes reward hacking via proxy/true-reward correlation and proposes a regularizer to mitigate it — the optimization-side framing of the failure this paper monitors for.

title: On Measuring Faithfulness or Self-consistency of Natural Language Explanations
why: Disentangles faithfulness from self-consistency in model explanations — sharpens the faithfulness-vs-monitorability distinction the paper builds its safety argument on.

title: Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs that Persist Through Safety Training
why: AF's direct predecessor — backdoored 'deceptive' models that survive safety training; the deceptive-behavior baseline AF's unprompted result is measured against.

title: Risks from Learned Optimization in Advanced Machine Learning Systems
why: The mesa-optimization / deceptive-alignment foundation AF's threat model rests on — defines the 'deceptively aligned' inner optimizer the paper empirically demonstrates.

title: Training large language models on narrow tasks can lead to broad misalignment
why: 'Emergent misalignment' — finetuning on a narrow bad behavior (insecure code) generalizes to broadly misaligned values; an influential safety result that cites AF.

title: Scheming AIs: Will AIs fake alignment during training in order to get power?
why: The report that names and argues for AF's exact scenario; AF cites it as the conceptual source of its threat model — pairs the theory with AF's empirics.

title: Subliminal Learning: Language models transmit behavioral traits via hidden signals in data
why: Models transmit behavioral traits through data with no semantic trace — a hidden-channel safety result (Truthful AI / Anthropic Fellows) that cites AF.

title: Measuring Faithfulness in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
why: Anthropic CoT-faithfulness study; load-bearing for whether CoT monitoring (the scheming follow-up thread) can be trusted.

title: Stress-Testing Capability Elicitation with Password-Locked Models
why: Sandbagging / capability-elicitation foundation directly underpinning the scheming sandbagging eval.

title: Towards evaluations-based safety cases for AI scheming
why: Apollo's companion safety-case framework that scheming evals feed into; defines the inability-argument this paper attacks.

title: Uncovering Deceptive Tendencies in Language Models: A Simulated Company AI Assistant
why: Direct precursor: a simulated-company agentic setup eliciting deception, which the scheming suite generalizes into six structured evals.

title: Evaluating Frontier Models for Stealth and Situational Awareness
why: Source of the oversight_pattern stealth environment and the safety-case scaffold Emmons extends; GDM dangerous-capability evals.

title: CoT Red-Handed: Stress Testing Chain-of-Thought Monitoring
why: The closest concurrent work — independent stress-testing of CoT monitoring; directly comparable methodology.

title: An Approach to Technical AGI Safety and Security
why: GDM's safety agenda; the stress-testing guidelines (Sec 6.7) Emmons applies are drawn from here.

title: Preventing Language Models From Hiding Their Reasoning
why: Early treatment of encoded/steganographic reasoning — the threat model behind the cipher/disguise attacks Emmons stress-tests.

title: Ctrl-Z: Controlling AI Agents via Resampling
why: Redwood's agentic successor to AI Control: extends trusted monitoring + resampling protocols from single code submissions to multi-step agent trajectories.

title: A sketch of an AI control safety case
why: Shows how control evaluations feed a structured safety case for deploying an untrusted model; bridges AI Control to the safety-case literature.

title: Model evaluation for extreme risks
why: The dangerous-capability-evaluation framework AI Control operationalizes; widely cited across the safety/oversight cluster.

title: The alignment problem from a deep learning perspective
why: Foundational threat-model paper (misaligned power-seeking) underpinning the 'intentional subversion' assumption behind AI Control.

title: Adaptive Deployment of Untrusted LLMs Reduces Distributed Threats
why: Extends AI Control's micro-protocol to a macro-protocol that adapts deployment over time as evidence of subversion accumulates.

title: Get To The Point: Summarization with Pointer-Generator Networks
authors: Abigail See, Peter J. Liu, Christopher D. Manning
why: The pointer-generator/coverage summarization model Ziegler's copy-vs-abstract analysis (Figs 5-7) is explicitly benchmarked against; canonical extractive-vs-abstractive reference.

title: Sequence Tutor: Conservative Fine-Tuning of Sequence Generation Models with KL-control
why: Origin of the KL-control penalty Ziegler ('following Jaques et al.') and all later RLHF use to keep the policy near the pretrained model.

title: Recursively Summarizing Books with Human Feedback
why: Direct RLHF descendant - scales the Ziegler/Stiennon reward-model+PPO recipe to book-length summarization via recursive task decomposition (OpenAI).

title: Sequence Level Training with Recurrent Neural Networks
why: Early RL-for-text-generation (MIXER) Ziegler cites as prior work optimizing sequence-level rewards (BLEU) directly.

title: A Deep Reinforced Model for Abstractive Summarization
authors: Romain Paulus, Caiming Xiong, Richard Socher
why: RL-with-ROUGE abstractive summarization Ziegler contrasts with; illustrates why programmatic rewards are weak proxies that human-preference reward models replace.

title: ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Development
why: Communicative multi-agent software-dev framework; a direct peer of the MetaGPT/GPT-Pilot/OpenHands developer agents benchmarked on DevAI.

title: Self-Play Fine-Tuning Converts Weak Language Models to Strong Language Models
why: Self-play weak-to-strong fine-tuning; concretizes the agentic self-improvement 'flywheel' Agent-as-a-Judge points to as future work.

title: MLLM-as-a-Judge: Assessing Multimodal LLM-as-a-Judge with Vision-Language Benchmark
why: Extends LLM-as-a-Judge to multimodal tasks; the nearest neighbor to Agent-as-a-Judge in the AI-judge lineage (MT-Bench, G-Eval).

title: CAMEL: Communicative Agents for "Mind" Exploration of Large Language Model Society
why: Foundational role-play multi-agent framework; shared KAUST lineage (Khizbullin, Ghanem) and an early instance of agents evaluating/steering agents.

title: Humans or LLMs as the Judge? A Study on Judgement Biases
why: Systematic study of LLM-judge biases; sharpens the reliability critique of LLM/Agent-as-a-Judge and the human-consensus ground truth.

title: R2E-Gym: Procedural Environments and Hybrid Verifiers for Scaling Open-Weights SWE Agents
why: Direct open-weights SWE-agent comparison (R2E-Gym-32B, 34.4% Verified); procedural env construction + hybrid verifiers, the inference-scaling counterpart to SWE-smith's data scaling.

title: Lingma SWE-GPT: An Open Development-Process-Centric Language Model for Automated Software Improvement
why: Open SWE model (Lingma-SWE-GPT-72B) baseline in SWE-smith's results table; development-process-centric training from Alibaba.

title: RePOST: Scalable Repository-Level Coding Environment Construction with Sandbox Testing
why: Concurrent work to SWE-smith: automatic repo-level execution-environment construction by sandboxing functions + LM-generated tests, a contrasting route to scalable SWE data.

title: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Program Better
authors: Patrick Haluptzok, Matthew Bowers, Adam Tauman Kalai
why: Conceptual basis SWE-smith cites: an LM generates and self-verifies programming problems via execution; SWE-smith applies this at repository scale by having an LM break codebases.

title: A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents
why: The canonical survey of LLM-based autonomous agents (construction, evaluation, applications); a high-citation hub the agents cluster broadly cites.

title: Data Interpreter: An LLM Agent For Data Science
why: A data-science research agent (plan→code→reflect over ML pipelines) — the closest sibling to MLGym's ML-research tasks.

title: Magentic-One: A Generalist Multi-Agent System for Solving Complex Tasks
why: Microsoft's orchestrator-led generalist multi-agent system; a strong scaffolding baseline MLGym-style frameworks integrate and evaluate.

title: SWE-Search: Enhancing Software Agents with Monte Carlo Tree Search and Iterative Refinement
why: Adds MCTS + self-reflection to software agents on SWE-bench; connects MLGym to the existing SWE-bench/SWE-agent/OpenHands/Agentless cluster.

title: Benchmarking Optimization Software with Performance Profiles
authors: Elizabeth D. Dolan, Jorge J. Moré
why: The performance-profile method (Dolan & Moré) that MLGym's AUP score is built on — the methodological root of fair cross-task agent comparison.

title: Using DeepSpeed and Megatron to Train Megatron-Turing NLG 530B, A Large-Scale Generative Language Model
why: The 530B dense LM PaLM benchmarks against — the largest dense model before PaLM and a key pre-PaLM scaling baseline.

title: Pathways: Asynchronous Distributed Dataflow for ML
why: The Pathways system PaLM is built on (the 'Barham et al. 2022' cited throughout) — the infrastructure enabling pipeline-free 540B training across two TPU v4 pods.

title: Unified Scaling Laws for Routed Language Models
why: DeepMind scaling laws for routed/MoE LMs; Chinchilla cites it for the compute-vs-size power-law assumption.

title: Scaling Laws for Transfer
why: How pretraining compute transfers to data-limited fine-tuning; part of the scaling-law cluster Chinchilla builds on.

title: Pre-train, Prompt, and Predict: A Systematic Survey of Prompting Methods in Natural Language Processing
why: The canonical survey that named the 'pre-train, prompt, predict' paradigm; the reference map for the prompting/in-context-learning era FLAN sits in.

title: Surface Form Competition: Why the Highest Probability Answer Isn't Always Right
why: Shows naive likelihood scoring of multiple-choice answers is biased by surface form, motivating PMI/normalized scoring — directly relevant to how FLAN scores classification options.

title: OPT-IML: Scaling Language Model Instruction Meta Learning through the Lens of Generalization
why: Meta's instruction-tuning study scaling to ~2,000 tasks; a direct FLAN successor that systematizes instruction-tuning generalization and cites FLAN throughout.

title: Are DeepSeek R1 And Other Reasoning Models More Faithful?
why: The concurrent study chen-2025 most directly compares against; same hint-based methodology, finds reasoning models more-but-not-fully faithful.

title: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning In The Wild Is Not Always Faithful
why: Concurrent faithfulness work chen-2025 discusses — characterizes restoration errors and unfaithful shortcuts in reasoning-model CoT.

title: Auditing Language Models for Hidden Objectives
why: The out-of-context-reasoning / hidden-objective auditing work chen-2025 cites as the harder regime its MCQ setting cannot reach.

title: Question Decomposition Improves the Faithfulness of Model-Generated Reasoning
why: Anthropic decomposition method that boosts CoT faithfulness; a constructive counterpoint to chen-2025's negative results.

title: Qwen2.5-Coder Technical Report
why: The coding specialization whose instruction data is seeded into Qwen2.5 pretraining; pairs with the already-queued Qwen2.5-Math to complete the Qwen2.5 specialist family.

title: Gemma 2: Improving Open Language Models at a Practical Size
why: Google's open-weight baseline and a recurring head-to-head comparison point across the graph's model reports (Qwen2.5, Llama, Mistral).

title: Yi: Open Foundation Models by 01.AI
why: 01.AI's open foundation family (Yi-1.5-34B) used as a mid-size baseline in Qwen2.5's evaluations; a notable bilingual open-weight model report.

title: Phi-3 Technical Report: A Highly Capable Language Model Locally on Your Phone
why: Microsoft's data-quality-first small-model line (Phi-3.5-mini), the edge-side baseline Qwen2.5-3B is benchmarked against; a strong 'data over scale' counterpoint.

title: DeepSpeed-Chat: Easy, Fast and Affordable RLHF Training of ChatGPT-like Models at All Scales
why: The first widely-used open RLHF system and HybridFlow’s colocate-all baseline; the DeepSpeed/ZeRO-based reference design the open-RLHF wave reacted against.

title: OpenRLHF: An Easy-to-use, Scalable and High-performance RLHF Framework
why: The Ray-based RLHF framework and HybridFlow’s standalone-placement baseline; one of the two open frameworks (with verl) the RL-for-reasoning wave standardized on.

title: NeMo-Aligner: Scalable Toolkit for Efficient Model Alignment
why: NVIDIA’s 3D-parallel RLHF toolkit and HybridFlow’s split-placement baseline; the KVCache-less generation path HybridFlow shows is the bottleneck.

title: ReMax: A Simple, Effective, and Efficient Reinforcement Learning Method for Aligning Large Language Models
why: A critic-free RLHF algorithm (REINFORCE-with-baseline) HybridFlow runs as a dataflow variant; part of the PPO→GRPO→DAPO critic-elimination lineage on the map.

title: Ray: A Distributed Framework for Emerging AI Applications
why: The distributed task/actor framework HybridFlow’s single-controller is built on; foundational distributed-AI infrastructure cited across the RL-systems cluster.

title: Do NOT Think That Much for 2+3=? On the Overthinking of o1-Like LLMs
why: Names and measures the 'overthinking' failure mode Kimi's length penalty targets — motivates the token-efficiency / long2short thread in the reasoning cluster.

title: Mirror Descent Policy Optimization
why: The RL algorithm Kimi's critic-free policy optimization is a variant of; foundational to the online-mirror-descent branch of the post-training cluster.

title: Critique-out-Loud Reward Models
why: Reasoning-before-judging reward models — the design Kimi's 98.5%-accurate CoT RM follows; ties the RM-quality thread across the RL cluster.

title: LLM Critics Help Catch LLM Bugs
why: OpenAI's CriticGPT — generative critique as a reward/verification signal; cited by Kimi for CoT reward modeling, central to the verifier-quality discussion.

title: MAmmoTH: Building Math Generalist Models through Hybrid Instruction Tuning
why: A widely-cited math-reasoning model (CoT+PoT hybrid tuning); a math-reasoning anchor predating the RL-for-reasoning wave Kimi sits in.

title: Graph of Thoughts: Solving Elaborate Problems with Large Language Models
why: Inference-time reasoning as a graph of thoughts — the next step past Tree-of-Thoughts (already a node) in the deliberation-at-inference lineage Monkeys situates repeated sampling within.

title: Mixture-of-Agents Enhances Large Language Model Capabilities
why: Ensembling many LLMs at inference time — a sibling inference-compute-scaling approach to Monkeys' repeated sampling, and a strong connector to the verifier/ensemble cluster.

title: Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking
why: Token-level deliberation (the STaR self-teaching lineage) — the 'spend tokens thinking' axis of inference compute that complements Monkeys' 'spend samples' axis.

title: Hydragen: High-Throughput LLM Inference with Shared Prefixes
why: The same authors' inference-systems paper that makes repeated sampling cheap (shared-prefix attention) — the load-bearing systems case behind Monkeys' cost argument.

title: Scaling Scaling Laws with Board Games
why: The test-time-compute scaling-law precedent (Hex): performance scales predictably with tree-search compute — a direct ancestor of Monkeys' inference-time scaling laws.

title: Back to Basics: Revisiting REINFORCE Style Optimization for Learning from Human Feedback in LLMs
why: REINFORCE-Leave-One-Out (RLOO) — Dr. GRPO's unbiased advantage equals RLOO up to a scale factor; the critic-free baseline this paper's fix reduces to.

title: Cognitive Behaviors that Enable Self-Improving Reasoners, or, Four Habits of Highly Effective STaRs
why: The cognitive-behavior framework (Backtracking/Verification/Subgoal/Enumeration) that SimpleRL-Zoo's entire 'aha moment' analysis is built on.

title: Logic-RL: Unleashing LLM Reasoning with Rule-Based Reinforcement Learning
why: A sibling rule-based zero-RL reasoning reproduction directly compared against in the zero-RL wave.

title: Easy-to-Hard Generalization: Scalable Alignment Beyond Human Supervision
why: Difficulty-graded generalization in RL training — directly relevant to SimpleRL-Zoo's data-difficulty findings.

title: An Empirical Study on Eliciting and Improving R1-like Reasoning Models
why: A contemporaneous careful R1-Zero reproduction study; complements the zero-RL reproduction cluster.

title: Energy and Policy Considerations for Deep Learning in NLP
why: The carbon/energy accounting Stochastic Parrots leans on (the 284t CO2 and trans-American-flight figures); the source of the environmental-cost argument.

title: Language (Technology) is Power: A Critical Survey of “Bias” in NLP
why: The foundational survey of how 'bias' is (mis)defined in NLP; the conceptual backbone of the bias-amplification argument in Stochastic Parrots.

title: Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data
why: The octopus thought experiment and the form-vs-meaning argument that Stochastic Parrots builds its 'no understanding' thesis on.

title: Carbon Emissions and Large Neural Network Training
why: The direct rebuttal: argues the Strubell-style carbon estimates overstate routine training cost by ~1-2 orders of magnitude; the counterpoint to the Parrots environmental claim.

title: The Woman Worked as a Babysitter: On Biases in Language Generation
why: Early systematic study of social bias in open-ended LM generation; concrete evidence for the bias-harms Stochastic Parrots warns about.

title: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision
why: CLIP — named in the foundation-models definition; the canonical vision-language foundation model and a hub cited across the multimodal cluster.

title: Zero-Shot Text-to-Image Generation
why: DALL·E — the foundation-model paradigm applied to text-to-image generation; extends the graph beyond language/eval into generative multimodal.

title: wav2vec 2.0: A Framework for Self-Supervised Learning of Speech Representations
why: wav2vec 2.0 — the canonical self-supervised speech foundation model; fills the speech modality in the foundation-models map.

title: Decision Transformer: Reinforcement Learning via Sequence Modeling
why: Decision Transformer — recasts RL as sequence modeling, extending the foundation-model paradigm to control; links the RL cluster to the sequence-model lineage.

title: Crosslingual Generalization through Multitask Finetuning
why: BLOOMZ/mT0 — the multilingual successor to T0 from BigScience; extends multitask prompted finetuning to 46 languages and shows English-only prompts generalize crosslingually.

title: PromptSource: An Integrated Development Environment and Repository for Natural Language Prompts
why: T0's companion tooling paper — the IDE and repository used to build the Public Pool of Prompts (P3, 2,073 prompts across 177 datasets); the infrastructure behind multitask prompted training.

title: Do Prompt-Based Models Really Understand the Meaning of Their Prompts?
why: The prompting critique T0 cites — models often do nearly as well with irrelevant or misleading prompts, challenging the 'prompts as meaningful instructions' premise underlying instruction tuning.

title: State of What Art? A Call for Multi-Prompt LLM Evaluation
why: Generalizes T0's median-over-prompts robustness idea into an eval methodology — single-prompt LLM benchmarks are unreliable; evaluate across a distribution of prompt paraphrases instead.

title: How Abilities in Large Language Models are Affected by Supervised Fine-tuning Data Composition
why: Studies how instruction-tuning data composition (math, code, general) trades off abilities at scale — a direct empirical look at the data-mixture question T0's multitask mixture raises.

title: Social Simulacra: Creating Populated Prototypes for Social Computing Systems
why: Park's direct predecessor: an LLM generates believable social-media personas to prototype a community's emergent dynamics — the conceptual seed of generative agents.

title: Demonstrate-Search-Predict: Composing retrieval and language models for knowledge-intensive NLP
why: Retrieval+LM pipeline framework (DSP, the precursor to DSPy) that composes retrieve→generate steps for knowledge-intensive tasks; an early agentic-composition pattern.

title: Out of One, Many: Using Language Models to Simulate Human Samples
why: Evidence that an LLM conditioned on personas can reproduce human survey/sample distributions — the social-simulation thesis generative agents build on.

title: AgentVerse: Facilitating Multi-Agent Collaboration and Exploring Emergent Behaviors in Agents
why: A widely-cited multi-agent collaboration framework studying emergent group behavior; a prominent extension of the generative-agents line.

title: War and Peace (WarAgent): Large Language Model-based Multi-Agent Simulation of World Wars
why: High-profile GA-style social simulation: LLM agents enact historical world-war diplomacy, probing whether multi-agent sims reproduce real geopolitical dynamics.

title: PaLM-E: An Embodied Multimodal Language Model
why: Google's direct successor to SayCan — folds perception into the LLM itself as an embodied multimodal model; the next step in the grounded-LLM-agent lineage.

title: Code as Policies: Language Model Programs for Embodied Control
why: LLMs emit executable control code rather than ranked skills — an influential LLM-as-programmer agent paradigm that bridges SayCan to the coding-agent cluster.

title: Large Language Models Are Human-Level Prompt Engineers
why: APE — automatic prompt optimization, a foundational LLM method underpinning many agent/reasoning pipelines.

title: Augmented Language Models: a Survey
why: Canonical survey framing tool-use and reasoning augmentation of LLMs — the conceptual backbone of the agents cluster.

title: Socratic Models: Composing Zero-Shot Multimodal Reasoning with Language
why: Composes multiple pretrained models through language at inference time — an early modular multi-model LLM-agent idea concurrent with SayCan (shared author A. Zeng).

title: MineDojo: Building Open-Ended Embodied Agents with Internet-Scale Knowledge
why: The same team's foundational open-ended Minecraft benchmark + internet-scale knowledge base that Voyager is built on; the canonical embodied-agent simulator/dataset upstream of Voyager.

title: Video PreTraining (VPT): Learning to Act by Watching Unlabeled Online Videos
why: OpenAI's Minecraft foundation policy trained from unlabeled gameplay video; the gradient-based low-level controller Voyager calls 'orthogonal' and complementary to its LLM planner.

title: VIMA: General Robot Manipulation with Multimodal Prompts
why: Multimodal-prompted generalist robot agent from the Voyager/MineDojo lineage; a key embodied-agent reference for prompt-conditioned control.

title: Efficient Streaming Language Models with Attention Sinks
authors: Guangxuan Xiao, Yuandong Tian, Beidi Chen, Song Han, Mike Lewis
why: Cited by LoCoMo as prior long-context-handling work (StreamingLLM); ~1,179 citations clears the foundational bar -- background long-context efficiency work, not itself a benchmark.

title: Recipes for Building an Open-Domain Chatbot
authors: Stephen Roller, Emily Dinan, Naman Goyal, Da Ju, Mary Williamson, Yinhan Liu, Jing Xu, Myle Ott, Kurt Shuster, Eric M. Smith, Y-Lan Boureau, Jason Weston
why: BlenderBot / BST 2.7B -- the pre-trained model every architecture in this paper fine-tunes, and the sole external baseline in every results table.

title: Towards a Human-like Open-Domain Chatbot
authors: Daniel Adiwardana, Minh-Thang Luong, David R. So, Jamie Hall, Noah Fiedel, Romal Thoppilan, Zi Yang, Apoorv Kulshreshtha, Gaurav Nemade, Yifeng Lu, Quoc V. Le
why: Meena -- repeatedly cited alongside BlenderBot as the other state-of-the-art system motivating the paper's framing.

title: Memory Networks
authors: Jason Weston, Sumit Chopra, Antoine Bordes
why: Cited as the earlier-methods lineage retrieval-augmented dialogue descends from; old, foundational, very highly cited background work.

title: Prioritized Experience Replay
why: Prioritized DQN is the strongest value-based baseline in A3C's Table 1 (463.6% mean) and one of the improvements A3C's conclusion suggests combining with its own Q-learning variants.

title: Dueling Network Architectures for Deep Reinforcement Learning
why: Dueling D-DQN is the third DQN-extension baseline in A3C's Table 1 comparison and shares Table 1's evaluation setup with the other two.

title: A Natural Policy Gradient
why: TRPO's 'Connections with Prior Work' section shows its own update recovers the natural policy gradient as a special case, and natural gradient is a direct experimental baseline in the paper.

title: Approximately Optimal Approximate Reinforcement Learning
why: Introduces conservative policy iteration and the improvement bound (Eq. 6) that Theorem 1 in TRPO directly extends from mixture policies to general stochastic policies — TRPO's core theoretical predecessor.

title: Natural Actor-Critic
why: Referenced in TRPO's discussion of prior policy-update schemes unified by the trust-region perspective; a standard baseline in the policy-gradient lineage.

title: The Risk of Automation for Jobs in OECD Countries: A Comparative Analysis
authors: Melanie Arntz, Terry Gregory, Ulrich Zierahn
venue: OECD Social, Employment and Migration Working Papers
why: The direct task-level rebuttal this paper's own limitations section invites — recomputes automation risk using PIAAC task-composition data instead of whole occupations and finds only ~9% of jobs at high risk, versus this paper's 47%.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Jobs: Evidence from Online Vacancies
authors: Daron Acemoglu, David Autor, Jonathon Hazell, Pascual Restrepo
why: Measures AI exposure directly from employer job-vacancy text rather than O*NET task proxies — a market-revealed alternative to this paper's classifier-based occupation ranking.

title: The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market
why: Builds an occupation-level AI-exposure index from patent-text overlap with O*NET job tasks — the patent-based sibling to this paper's O*NET-classifier approach and Eloundou et al.'s LLM-rubric approach.

title: Certifying and Removing Disparate Impact
why: Cited directly as [FFM+15] — formalizes the legal '80% rule' disparate-impact test that this paper positions demographic parity's ratio-based cousin against.

title: Learning Fair Representations
why: Cited directly as [ZWS+13] — the representation-learning approach to demographic parity that this paper's post-processing method is contrasted against.

title: Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation
why: Foundational parameter-efficient finetuning method (learned prefix vectors) that predates LoRA; part of the PEFT lineage QLoRA cites directly in its related work.

title: HuggingFace's Transformers: State-of-the-art Natural Language Processing
why: The open-source library QLoRA (and nearly every other LLM finetuning paper in this graph) is implemented on top of; a foundational infrastructure paper worth its own node.

title: SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for Large Language Models
why: Major activation-aware quantization method QLoRA's related work cites alongside LLM.int8() as the two leading outlier-handling approaches for LLM quantization.

title: AugMix: A Simple Data Processing Method to Improve Robustness and Uncertainty
authors: Hendrycks, Mu, Cubuk, Zoph, Gilmer, Lakshminarayanan
why: The standard augmentation method benchmarked against IMAGENET-C mCE in nearly every subsequent robustness paper; a direct Hendrycks follow-up built on this paper's own benchmark.

title: Measuring Robustness to Natural Distribution Shifts in Image Classification
authors: Taori, Dave, Shankar, Carlini, Recht, Schmidt
why: Tests whether IMAGENET-C-style synthetic corruption robustness predicts natural distribution-shift robustness (finds little to no transfer) — directly answers this paper's open question.

title: Q-learning
why: The foundational off-policy TD algorithm DQN directly extends with a neural function approximator; missing from the graph despite underpinning nearly every value-based RL paper already in it.

title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
why: AlexNet — the deep-conv-net breakthrough DQN's own architecture is built on; a foundational lineage paper still missing from the graph.

title: Visualizing Data using t-SNE
why: The dimensionality-reduction technique DQN uses directly to produce its representation-visualization figure; extremely widely cited outside RL too.

title: Gradient-based learning applied to document recognition
why: LeNet-5 — the convolutional-network paper DQN cites as the architectural template it builds its Atari conv-net on.

title: Benchmarking Deep Reinforcement Learning for Continuous Control
why: Established the rllab benchmark suite and the HalfCheetah/Hopper/Walker2d/Ant/Humanoid MuJoCo task family that OpenAI Gym and nearly all later continuous-control RL papers still measure against.

title: Double Q-learning
why: The original tabular Double Q-learning algorithm this paper generalizes to deep function approximation — the direct algorithmic ancestor of Double DQN, currently missing from both the graph and the queue.

title: Massively Parallel Methods for Deep Reinforcement Learning
why: The distributed DQN variant (Gorila) this paper directly benchmarks against in its own no-op and human-starts result tables.

title: Deep Attention Recurrent Q-Network
why: A DQN-family extension adding a soft-attention mechanism over the convolutional feature map, cited directly in this paper's own reference list among contemporary DQN variants.

title: End-to-End Training of Deep Visuomotor Policies
why: Guided policy search — DDPG's related-work section compares its convolutional-policy architecture directly to this paper's; highly cited robotics/RL contemporary.

title: Learning Continuous Control Policies by Stochastic Value Gradients
authors: Heess, Wayne, Silver, Lillicrap, Tassa, Erez
why: SVG(0) — DDPG's related work calls DPG its deterministic limit; shares 5 of 8 authors with DDPG.

title: Memory-based control with recurrent neural networks
why: Directly cited by DDPG; extends the same actor-critic recipe to partially-observed/recurrent continuous control, sharing 3 authors.

title: Gradient Estimation Using Stochastic Computation Graphs
why: Cited in DDPG's references; formalizes the reparameterization-trick gradient estimators underlying stochastic-policy analogs like SVG(0) that DDPG's related work discusses.

title: Adapted large language models can outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization
authors: Van Veen, Van Uden, Blankemeier, Delbrouck, et al.
why: Highly-cited real-world clinical-LLM evaluation cited directly in the system card's Health section as the applied counterpart to its MedQA/MMLU numbers.

title: Managing extreme AI risks amid rapid progress
authors: Bengio, Hinton, Yao, Song, et al.
why: Prominent cross-institutional AI-risk position paper cited in the system card's societal-impacts discussion; foundational framing for catastrophic-risk evaluation work.

title: Capabilities of Gemini Models in Medicine
authors: Saab, Tu, Weng, Tanno, et al.
why: Med-Gemini paper the system card directly benchmarks GPT-4o's medical performance against (84.0% MedQA vs. GPT-4o's 89.4%).

title: Sociotechnical Safety Evaluation of Generative AI Systems
authors: Weidinger, Rauh, Marchal, Manzini, et al.
why: DeepMind's sociotechnical safety-evaluation framework, cited twice in the system card's risk/impact discussion; a candidate for the evaluations survey given its methodological focus.

title: Evaluating the Social Impact of Generative AI Systems in Systems and Society
authors: Solaiman, Talat, Agnew, Ahmad, et al.
why: Widely-cited multi-institution framework for evaluating generative-AI societal impact, cited in the system card's risk-identification section.

title: Bottom-Up and Top-Down Attention for Image Captioning and Visual Question Answering
authors: Peter Anderson, Xiaodong He, Chris Buehler, Damien Teney, Mark Johnson, Stephen Gould, Lei Zhang
why: The 2017 VQA Challenge-winning attention architecture that directly cites VQA v2 and substantially closed the language-prior gap this paper documents.

title: Visual Genome: Connecting Language and Vision Using Crowdsourced Dense Image Annotations
why: Directly referenced by VQA v2 as a companion large-scale vision-language annotation dataset; a major benchmark-family hub for grounding/region-QA work.

title: LXMERT: Learning Cross-Modality Encoder Representations from Transformers
why: Foundational vision-language transformer pretraining paper that cites VQA v2 and reports VQA v2 as a headline benchmark.

title: Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition
why: VGGNet — the actual fc7 embedding VQA v2's own methodology depends on to mine nearest-neighbor complementary images; a foundational CV backbone not yet in the graph.

title: Yin and Yang: Balancing and Answering Binary Visual Questions
authors: Peng Zhang, Yash Goyal, Douglas Summers-Stay, Dhruv Batra, Devi Parikh
why: The direct predecessor this paper explicitly differentiates against (binary-only questions on clipart abstract scenes) — necessary context for VQA v2's balancing methodology.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Deep Energy-Based Policies
why: SAC's own direct algorithmic predecessor (soft Q-learning) and one of its four experimental baselines; same lead author/lab.

title: Trust-PCL: An Off-Policy Trust Region Method for Continuous Control
why: Off-policy max-entropy trust-region baseline SAC compares against directly in its own appendix ablation.

title: Q-Prop: Sample-Efficient Policy Gradient with An Off-Policy Critic
why: Cited hybrid on/off-policy method; co-authored by Levine and Lillicrap, extending the same DDPG/SAC lineage already in the graph.
