title: A Distributional Perspective on Reinforcement Learning
why: Foundational distributional-RL paper (C51) cited in TD3's related work; spawned the QR-DQN/IQN/Rainbow lineage.

title: Distributed Distributional Deterministic Policy Gradients
authors: Barth-Maron, Hoffman, Budden, Dabney, Horgan, TB, Muldal, Heess, Lillicrap
why: Direct distributional extension of the DDPG/TD3 continuous-control lineage, cited in TD3's related work.

title: IMPALA: Scalable Distributed Deep-RL with Importance Weighted Actor-Learner Architectures
authors: Espeholt, Soyer, Munos, Simonyan, Mnih, et al.
why: Highly-cited distributed-RL architecture cited in TD3's related-work discussion of distributed methods.

title: Distributed Prioritized Experience Replay
authors: Horgan, Quan, Budden, Barth-Maron, Hessel, van Hasselt, Silver
why: Cited in TD3's related work on distributed/prioritized-replay improvements to DDPG-style methods.

title: ViZDoom: A Doom-based AI Research Platform for Visual Reinforcement Learning
why: The engine behind Gym's Doom-based environments added after the initial release.

title: DeepMind Control Suite
authors: Tassa, Doron, Muldal, Erez, Li, de Las Casas, Budden, Abdolmaleki, Merel, Lefrancq, Lillicrap, Riedmiller
why: The MuJoCo-based competing/complementary continuous-control benchmark suite from DeepMind, frequently paired with Gym in the RL-benchmarks literature.

title: Gymnasium: A Standard Interface for Reinforcement Learning Environments
authors: Towers, Kwiatkowski, Terry, Balis, De Cola, Deleu, Goulão, Kallinteris, Krimmel, KG, Perez-Vicente, Pierré, Schulhoff, Tai, Tan, Younis
why: The Farama Foundation's formal paper for Gymnasium, the maintained fork/successor to Gym after OpenAI stopped active maintenance in 2020.

title: Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning
why: OpenAI's own Gym-interface-compatible follow-on benchmark suite for procedural generalization; shares an author (Schulman) with Gym.

title: WikiQA: A Challenge Dataset for Open-Domain Question Answering
why: Peer 2015 QA benchmark (answer-sentence-selection format) TriviaQA directly compares itself against in its Table 1 dataset survey.

title: SearchQA: A New Q&A Dataset Augmented with Context from a Search Engine
authors: Dunn, Sagun, Higgins, Guney, Cirik, Cho
why: Sibling open-domain QA dataset (Jeopardy questions + search-engine snippets) discussed in both TriviaQA's and HotpotQA's related work as a peer multi-document dataset.

title: MCTest: A Challenge Dataset for the Open-Domain Machine Comprehension of Text
why: The earliest natural-language RC dataset TriviaQA's related work sizes itself against; foundational to the naturally-worded-question RC lineage.

title: Baichuan 2: Open Large-scale Language Models
why: The direct bilingual open-model competitor Qwen benchmarks against throughout; a parallel Chinese-lab base+chat family report.

title: Extending Context Window of Large Language Models via Positional Interpolation
why: The position-interpolation method Qwen's own NTK-aware context extension explicitly contrasts itself against; foundational for the long-context-extension line of work.

title: CMMLU: Measuring massive multitask language understanding in Chinese
why: The Chinese-language MMLU counterpart used to evaluate Qwen's bilingual capability; a named benchmark entry for a future benchmarks survey.

title: YaRN: Efficient Context Window Extension of Large Language Models
why: Cited as the formalization of the 'dynamic NTK-aware interpolation' technique Qwen implements ad hoc; became a widely-adopted long-context method in later open models.

title: RRHF: Rank Responses to Align Language Models with Human Feedback without tears
why: An RLHF alternative (ranking loss instead of PPO) contemporaneous with Qwen's PPO pipeline; a precursor to the DPO-style ranking objectives already in the graph.

title: A Thorough Examination of the CNN/Daily Mail Reading Comprehension Task
why: The direct critique paper — hand-annotated error analysis showing ~1-in-4 CNN test questions are coref-broken/unanswerable and that a tuned attention reader is already near the effective ceiling; cited by both TriviaQA and CNN/Daily Mail's own reference lists.

title: NewsQA: A Machine Comprehension Dataset
authors: Trischler, Wang, Yuan, Harris, Sordoni, Bachman, Suleman
why: Direct successor RC dataset (also CNN-sourced) explicitly designed to fix CNN/Daily Mail's answer-salience shortcuts; cited by both TriviaQA and CNN/Daily Mail's own reference lists.

title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate
why: The attention mechanism this paper's Attentive Reader explicitly draws on; foundational, extremely highly cited.

title: Constructing Datasets for Multi-hop Reading Comprehension Across Documents (QAngaroo: WikiHop + MedHop)
why: Extends the single-document cloze RC paradigm this paper started to multi-hop, multi-document reasoning.

title: End-To-End Memory Networks
why: Memory-augmented QA precedent cited by this paper; companion to the already-queued Weston et al. Memory Networks, rounding out that lineage.

title: Automation and New Tasks: How Technology Displaces and Reinstates Labor
why: The task-displacement/reinstatement theory this paper's model is built on — quantifies the mechanism whose destructive half alone 'Robots and Jobs' measures.

title: Demographics and Automation
why: Establishes the demographic mechanism underwriting the validity of this paper's European robot-adoption instrument for US exposure.

title: The Growth of Low-Skill Service Jobs and the Polarization of the US Labor Market
why: The foundational routine-task-automation/labor-polarization paper this paper's occupation-level results sit inside.

title: PromptBench: Towards Evaluating the Robustness of Large Language Models on Adversarial Prompts
why: Robustness/adversarial-prompt benchmark cited repeatedly by this survey; same author group as the survey.

title: GLUE-X: Evaluating Natural Language Understanding Models from an Out-of-Distribution Generalization Perspective
why: OOD-generalization benchmark cited in the survey's general-benchmarks table; extends GLUE/SuperGLUE to distribution shift.

title: Xiezhi: An Ever-Updating Benchmark for Holistic Domain Knowledge Evaluation
why: Ever-updating, holistic domain-knowledge benchmark cited in the survey; its living-benchmark design directly targets the survey's own 'dynamic evaluation' grand challenge.

title: KoLA: Carefully Benchmarking World Knowledge of Large Language Models
why: World-knowledge benchmark cited in the survey, complementing MMLU/AGIEval already in the graph.

title: Revisiting Out-of-distribution Robustness in NLP: Benchmark, Analysis, and LLMs Evaluations
why: OOD-robustness benchmark suite (BOSS) cited in the survey.

title: Fairness Constraints: Mechanisms for Fair Classification
authors: Muhammad Bilal Zafar, Isabel Valera, Manuel Gomez Rodriguez, Krishna P. Gummadi
why: Operationalizes fairness as a decision-boundary-covariance constraint enforced at training time — the first widely-used constructive alternative to Dwork et al.'s Lipschitz/metric approach.

title: A Reductions Approach to Fair Classification
authors: Alekh Agarwal, Alina Beygelzimer, Miroslav Dudík, John Langford, Hanna Wallach
why: Reduces fair classification under demographic parity or equalized odds to a sequence of cost-sensitive problems — the standard practical training-time recipe.

title: Counterfactual Fairness
authors: Matt J. Kusner, Joshua R. Loftus, Chris Russell, Ricardo Silva
why: Replaces Dwork et al.'s assumed similarity metric with an explicit causal model — the causal-inference answer to the same metric-legitimacy problem.

title: On the (Im)Possibility of Fairness
authors: Sorelle A. Friedler, Carlos Scheidegger, Suresh Venkatasubramanian
why: Formalizes why individual-fairness and group-fairness criteria are only reconcilable under specific 'worldview' assumptions — the field's clearest account of why fairness definitions proliferate and conflict.

title: Atari-5: Distilling the Arcade Learning Environment down to Five Games
why: Directly engages ALE's own evaluation-protocol legacy, distilling the 50+ game suite to a 5-game proxy correlated with the full-suite score.

title: Craftax: A Lightning-Fast Benchmark for Open-Ended Reinforcement Learning
authors: Matthews, Beukman, Ellis, Samvelyan, Jackson, Coward, Foerster
why: A JAX-native, open-ended successor to ALE's premise, built to run ~1000x faster for exploration research.

title: HumanoidBench: Simulated Humanoid Benchmark for Whole-Body Locomotion and Manipulation
why: A modern RL agent-evaluation-environment paper in ALE's direct lineage.

title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents
authors: Hu, Huang, Liu, Kompella, Ilhan, Tekin, Xu, Yahn, Liu
why: Traces ALE's 'games as agent evaluation' legacy into the LLM era.

title: BOLD: Dataset and Metrics for Measuring Biases in Open-Ended Language Generation
authors: Dhamala, Sun, Kumar, Krishna, Pruksachatkun, Chang, Gupta
why: Extends WEAT-style intrinsic bias measurement to open-ended generation with a dedicated benchmark dataset.

title: Underspecification Presents Challenges for Credibility in Modern Machine Learning
authors: D'Amour, Heller, Moldovan, Adlam, et al.
why: A broader eval-credibility argument that WEAT's construct-validity gap is a specific instance of.

title: Retiring Adult: New Datasets for Fair Machine Learning
why: Replaces the aging UCI Adult dataset with modern fairness benchmarks — the tabular-data counterpart to WEAT's word-embedding bias tests.

title: Marked Personas: Using Natural Language Prompts to Measure Stereotypes in Language Models
why: A direct methodological descendant: measures LLM stereotyping via generated personas, the generative-era heir to WEAT's association-test approach.

title: Data Statements for Natural Language Processing: Toward Mitigating System Bias and Enabling Better Science
why: The NLP-specific documentation precedent Datasheets explicitly discusses as a compatible proposal.

title: The Dataset Nutrition Label: A Framework To Drive Higher Data Quality Standards
why: A parallel dataset-documentation format discussed in this paper's impact section.

title: FactSheets: Increasing Trust in AI Services through Supplier's Declarations of Conformity
why: IBM's broader AI-service documentation analogue, cited in this paper's impact section.

title: Policy Gradient Methods for Reinforcement Learning with Function Approximation
why: Proves the policy-gradient theorem the textbook presents; the direct theoretical ancestor of every actor-critic method in this graph.

title: Neuronlike adaptive elements that can solve difficult learning control problems (actor-critic)
venue: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics
why: The original actor-critic paper — confirmed via TRPO's own reference list as TRPO's actual Sutton/Barto citation (not the textbook itself).

title: Temporal Difference Learning and TD-Gammon
why: The textbook's own headline case study for TD-learning at scale; the pre-deep-RL proof that self-play + TD learning can reach superhuman play.

title: Reuse, Don't Retrain: A Recipe for Continued Pretraining of Language Models
authors: Jupinder Parmar, Sanjev Satheesh, Mostofa Patwary, Mohammad Shoeybi, Bryan Catanzaro

title: Synthetic continued pretraining
authors: Zitong Yang, Neil Band, Shuangning Li, Emmanuel Candès, Tatsunori Hashimoto

title: D-CPT Law: Domain-specific Continual Pre-Training Scaling Law for Large Language Models
authors: Haoran Que, Jiaheng Liu, Ge Zhang, Chenchen Zhang, Xingwei Qu, et al.

title: Get more for less: Principled Data Selection for Warming Up Fine-Tuning in LLMs
authors: Feiyang Kang, Hoang Anh Just, Yifan Sun, Himanshu Jahagirdar, Yuanzhi Zhang, Rongxing Du, Anit Kumar Sahu, Ruoxi Jia
why: Directly builds on DAPT/TAPT's data-selection question: which data to prioritize for continued-pretraining warm-up.

title: Long Short-Term Memory
why: The recurrent-memory architecture underlying nearly every 2014-2015 neural captioning model this evaluation server was built to score — foundational and still missing from the graph.

title: Long-term Recurrent Convolutional Networks for Visual Recognition and Description
authors: Donahue, Hendricks, Rohrbach, Venugopalan, Guadarrama, Saenko, Darrell

why: Introduces Flickr30k and the denotation-graph framing this paper cites by name as the direct model for its own AMT caption-collection protocol.

title: Unifying Visual-Semantic Embeddings with Multimodal Neural Language Models
why: Kiros et al.'s multimodal embedding + LSTM decoder — one of the contemporaneous neural captioning systems in this paper's own related-work list.

title: Framing Image Description as a Ranking Task: Data, Models and Evaluation Metrics
why: Introduces Flickr8k and the AMT captioning protocol this paper explicitly credits as the model for its own data-collection methodology.

authors: Jia, Yang, Xia, Chen, Parekh, Pham, Le, Sung, Li, Duerig

title: ViLBERT: Pretraining Task-Agnostic Visiolinguistic Representations for Vision-and-Language Tasks

title: TIGEr: Text-to-Image Grounding for Image Caption Evaluation

why: A direct reference+image-grounding caption-eval-metric predecessor, reported as a baseline in every CLIPScore correlation table.

title: UMIC: An Unreferenced Metric for Image Captioning via Contrastive Learning
why: The contemporaneous reference-free caption metric CLIPScore explicitly contrasts itself against, trained with synthetic hard-negative captions.

title: Evaluating CLIP: Towards Characterization of Broader Capabilities and Downstream Implications
authors: Agarwal, Krueger, Clark, Radford, Kim, Brundage
why: The bias-audit of CLIP itself that CLIPScore's own limitations section cites — directly relevant to CLIP-S's circularity/bias risk as a judge.

authors: Geirhos, Medina Temme, Rauber, Schütt, Bethge, Wichmann

title: A Study and Comparison of Human and Deep Learning Recognition Performance under Visual Distortions
why: Early human-vs-CNN distortion-robustness comparison motivating this paper's robustness experiments.

title: Comparing deep neural networks against humans: object recognition when the signal gets weaker
authors: Geirhos, Janssen, Schütt, Rauber, Bethge, Wichmann
why: Same-lab precursor establishing the psychophysical human-CNN comparison methodology this paper builds on.

title: Cognitive Psychology for Deep Neural Networks: A Shape Bias Case Study
why: Direct precursor on measuring shape bias in neural networks; this paper explicitly engages with and extends its claims.

title: Histograms of Oriented Gradients for Human Detection

title: LabelMe: A Database and Web-Based Tool for Image Annotation
why: One of the six core datasets in this paper's cross-dataset generalization study; a foundational scene-annotation dataset/tool from the same MIT lab.

title: SUN Database: Large-scale scene recognition from abbey to zoo
why: Another of the six core cross-dataset generalization datasets in this paper; a large-scale scene-recognition benchmark built by Torralba's own lab.

title: A discriminatively trained, multiscale, deformable part model
why: Cited as "the currently best-performing detector" of its era; the dominant pre-deep-learning object detector (DPM).

title: Caltech-256 Object Category Dataset
why: One of the twelve datasets in this paper's "Name That Dataset" experiment; companion/successor to Caltech-101.

title: Function Optimization using Connectionist Reinforcement Learning Algorithms

title: Recurrent Models of Visual Attention
why: The landmark deep-learning-era application of REINFORCE (hard visual attention) — carried the 1992 score-function estimator into modern neural networks.

title: Self-Critical Sequence Training for Image Captioning
why: A widely-used REINFORCE-with-baseline recipe for sequence generation — a direct modern descendant of the reinforcement-comparison idea.

title: Reinforcement learning of motor skills with policy gradients
why: The standard synthesis connecting episodic REINFORCE to natural actor-critic for robotic policy search.

title: Measuring and Mitigating Unintended Bias in Text Classification
authors: Lucas Dixon, John Li, Jeffrey Sorensen, Nithum Thain, Lucy Vasserman
why: Source of the Pinned AUC metric and the synthetic template-based test set used directly in Model Cards' own toxicity-classifier worked example.

title: Machine Bias: There's Software Used Across the Country to Predict Future Criminals. And It's Biased Against Blacks.

title: Fairness in Criminal Justice Risk Assessments: The State of the Art

title: A Theoretical Analysis of Contrastive Unsupervised Representation Learning

title: Show, Attend and Tell: Neural Image Caption Generation with Visual Attention

title: Qwen-VL: A Versatile Vision-Language Model for Understanding, Localization, Text Reading, and Beyond

why: technical report behind the UI-Venus-7B/72B baselines evaluated in MobileWorld's Table 6

title: Prediction-Powered Ranking of Large Language Models
why: statistically-grounded successor to Elo-based LLM ranking, the most-cited of the post-Elo-Uncovered ranking-reliability papers

title: am-ELO: A Stable Framework for Arena-based LLM Evaluation
why: proposes a stability fix for arena-style Elo, the natural methodological follow-up to this paper's diagnosis

title: Ranking Unraveled: Recipes for LLM Rankings in Head-to-Head AI Combat
why: practical recipes for LLM head-to-head ranking reliability, building directly on this paper's reliability/transitivity axioms

title: A Unified Perturbation Framework for Analyzing Leaderboard Stability and Manipulation
why: very recent, thematically identical successor formally analyzing leaderboard stability and manipulation

title: Convergence and stationary distribution of Elo rating systems
why: theoretical convergence analysis of the exact Elo dynamics this paper stress-tests empirically

title: The False Promise of Imitating Proprietary LLMs
why: Directly cited in this paper's related-work gap between user perception and standardized eval suites; foundational critique of imitation-model evaluation quality.

title: Systematic Evaluation of LLM-as-a-Judge in LLM Alignment Tasks: Explainable Metrics and Diverse Prompt Templates
why: Extends the judge-bias-audit theme with explainable metrics for LLM-as-judge alignment evaluation.

title: Style Outweighs Substance: Failure Modes of LLM Judges in Alignment Benchmarking
why: Near-identically-themed direct successor cataloguing further LLM-judge failure modes; cites this paper.

title: Skewed Score: A statistical framework to assess autograders
why: Proposes the statistical rigor (significance testing, confidence intervals) this paper's own critique flags as missing from LLM-judge/autograder bias audits.

title: Quantifying Language Models' Sensitivity to Spurious Features in Prompt Design or: How I learned to start worrying about prompt formatting
why: Highest-cited unmatched reference; shows benchmark accuracy is highly sensitive to prompt formatting, directly relevant to evaluation robustness for any efficient/IRT-anchor method.

title: LMMs-Eval: Reality Check on the Evaluation of Large Multimodal Models
why: Major standardized efficient-evaluation tooling paper for multimodal models, citing tinyBenchmarks; same efficient-benchmarking lineage.

title: Anchor Points: Benchmarking Models with Much Fewer Examples
authors: Rajan Vivek, Kawin Ethayarajh, Diyi Yang, Douwe Kiela
why: The direct correctness-clustering baseline method tinyBenchmarks builds on and compares against throughout — a surprising gap in the graph given how central it is to this paper's method.

title: Efficient multi-prompt evaluation of LLMs
why: Same lead-author follow-up extending tinyBenchmarks' efficient-evaluation approach to multi-prompt evaluation.

title: Lost in Benchmarks? Rethinking Large Language Model Benchmarking with Item Response Theory
why: Direct IRT-based-benchmarking descendant, closing the IRT-based efficient-evaluation family gap this paper opened.

title: Membership Inference Attacks Against Machine Learning Models
why: The original membership-inference-attack paper (5439 citations) that the survey's entire MIA detection family builds on.

title: xCOMET: Transparent Machine Translation Evaluation through Fine-grained Error Detection
why: The reference-based metric that wins the WMT23 Metrics Shared Task outright (0.825 avg. correlation) — the paper's own headline result.

title: Error Analysis Prompting Enables Human-Like Translation Evaluation in Large Language Models: A Case Study on ChatGPT
why: The LLM-prompting error-analysis approach behind GEMBA-MQM, which placed among the top entries in WMT23 (0.802 avg. correlation).

title: Findings of the 2023 Conference on Machine Translation (WMT23): LLMs Are Here but Not Quite There Yet
why: The parent WMT23 General Translation shared task whose system outputs and human ratings this metrics paper validates against.

title: MultiWOZ - A Large-Scale Multi-Domain Wizard-of-Oz Dataset for Task-Oriented Dialogue Modelling
authors: Paweł Budzianowski, Tsung-Hsien Wen, Bo-Hsiang Tseng, Iñigo Casanueva, Stefan Ultes, Osman Ramadan, Milica Gasic
why: The foundational multi-domain task-oriented dialogue dataset the survey cites as anchoring the conversational-agents lineage that led to τ-Bench.

title: Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet
authors: Jie Huang, Xinyun Chen, Swaroop Mishra, Huaixiu Steven Zheng, Adams Wei Yu, Xinying Song, Denny Zhou
why: Highly-cited empirical finding that LLMs can't reliably self-correct without external feedback — the paper behind the survey's flagged gap that self-reflection evaluation still lacks a standardized benchmark.

title: A-MEM: Agentic Memory for LLM Agents
authors: Wujiang Xu, Zujie Liang, Kai Mei, Hang Gao, Juntao Tan, Yongfeng Zhang
why: A named agentic-memory method cited directly in the survey's memory-evaluation section — one of the newest entries in a fast-moving sub-area.

title: LongMemEval: Benchmarking Chat Assistants on Long-Term Interactive Memory
authors: Di Wu, Hongwei Wang, Wenhao Yu, Yuwei Zhang, Kai-Wei Chang, Dong Yu
why: A dedicated long-term memory benchmark cited by both the agent-eval survey and LongBench v2's dialogue-history task category.

title: A Reduction of Imitation Learning and Structured Prediction to No-Regret Online Learning
authors: Stéphane Ross, Geoffrey J. Gordon, J. Andrew Bagnell
why: DAgger — the imitation-learning alternative to reward optimization that Skalse et al.'s own discussion names as a way to sidestep reward hacking entirely.

title: Policy Invariance Under Reward Transformations: Theory and Application to Reward Shaping
authors: Andrew Y. Ng, Daishi Harada, Stuart J. Russell
why: The classical reward-shaping / policy-invariance theory that Skalse et al.'s 'unhackability' formalism directly extends and generalizes.

title: Algorithms for Inverse Reinforcement Learning
authors: Andrew Y. Ng, Stuart J. Russell
why: The foundational inverse-RL paper — learning a reward function from behavior is exactly the 'proxy reward' pipeline whose hackability this paper formalizes.

title: Learning Robust Rewards with Adversarial Inverse Reinforcement Learning
authors: Justin Fu, Katie Luo, Sergey Levine
why: AIRL, a widely-used learned-reward method of exactly the kind Skalse et al.'s results say is almost certainly hackable on the full policy set.

title: Cooperative Inverse Reinforcement Learning
authors: Dylan Hadfield-Menell, Stuart J. Russell, Pieter Abbeel, Anca Dragan
why: The cooperative-game formalization of reward misspecification and value alignment that sits alongside Skalse et al.'s hackability framework in the reward-design literature.

title: Large Language Model based Multi-Agents: A Survey of Progress and Challenges
why: Highly-cited (1,037) survey of the LLM-multi-agent literature API-Bank sits within.

title: Large Language Models as Tool Makers
why: Referenced in API-Bank's related work (LATM); lets an LLM synthesize its own new tools rather than just call existing APIs.

title: Tool learning with large language models: a survey
why: Field-overview survey of tool learning, the area API-Bank helped originate; cites API-Bank directly.

title: TaskMatrix.AI: Completing Tasks by Connecting Foundation Models with Millions of APIs
why: Early (2023-03) vision paper for connecting LLMs to millions of APIs, named in API-Bank's own related work.

title: EASYTOOL: Enhancing LLM-based Agents with Concise Tool Instruction
why: Post-API-Bank tool-instruction-design paper citing API-Bank's taxonomy; representative of the field's later direction.

title: NYU CTF Bench: A Scalable Open-Source Benchmark Dataset for Evaluating LLMs in Offensive Security
why: Cybench's own named concurrent-work comparison — a university-level (CSAW) open CTF benchmark it explicitly contrasts itself against.

title: Teams of LLM Agents can Exploit Zero-Day Vulnerabilities
why: Highly-cited paper on LLM-agent teams autonomously exploiting real zero-day vulnerabilities, cited in Cybench's own introduction.

title: CVE-Bench: A Benchmark for AI Agents' Ability to Exploit Real-World Web Application Vulnerabilities
why: A CTF/exploit-benchmark descendant in Cybench's lineage — agents exploiting real-world CVEs in web applications.

title: When LLMs meet cybersecurity: a systematic literature review
why: Systematic literature review mapping the LLM-cybersecurity space Cybench sits within.

title: HackSynth: LLM Agent and Evaluation Framework for Autonomous Penetration Testing
authors: Lajos Muzsai, David Imolai, András Lukács
why: LLM agent + evaluation framework for autonomous penetration testing — a close cousin benchmark in the offensive-cyber-agent space.

title: An Explanation of In-context Learning as Implicit Bayesian Inference
why: Foundational theory of why in-context/few-shot prompting works at all — the closest thing BBH's answer-only vs CoT comparison has to a mechanistic explanation.

title: Can language models learn from explanations in context?
why: Direct predecessor on whether explanations/reasoning traces in the prompt improve task performance, the same question BBH answers empirically at scale.

title: Binding Language Models in Symbolic Languages
why: Binder — tool/symbolic-augmented reasoning contemporaneous with BBH, part of the same 2022 wave of "prompting alone underestimates capability" results.

title: Large Language Models are few(1)-shot Table Reasoners
why: Referenced alongside BBH as another case where standard few-shot prompting undersold LLM reasoning on a structured task family (tables).

title: Text and Patterns: For Effective Chain of Thought, It Takes Two to Tango
why: Analyzes which components of a CoT prompt (text vs. computational pattern) actually drive the gains BBH documents empirically.

title: Universal Intelligence: A Definition of Machine Intelligence
why: The 2007 definition-of-intelligence survey Chollet repeatedly cites and builds on/against ("Intelligence measures an agent's ability to achieve goals in a wide range of environments") — the direct intellectual predecessor.

title: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models
why: Uses ARC as its core evaluation domain for LLM-driven program synthesis — a direct downstream application of Chollet's benchmark.

title: ARC-AGI-2: A New Challenge for Frontier AI Reasoning Systems
why: Chollet's own 2025 sequel benchmark, released once ARC-AGI-1 began saturating under heavy test-time-compute solvers — the direct successor to this paper's ARC.

title: Phenomenal Yet Puzzling: Testing Inductive Reasoning Capabilities of Language Models with Hypothesis Refinement
why: Inductive-reasoning benchmark in the same ARC-style few-shot-rule-induction spirit as Chollet's skill-acquisition-efficiency framing.

title: Combining Induction and Transduction for Abstract Reasoning
why: One of the strongest published ARC-solving approaches (MIT), directly engaging with the benchmark this paper introduced.

title: Improved Baselines with Visual Instruction Tuning
authors: Haotian Liu, Chunyuan Li, Yuheng Li, Yong Jae Lee
why: LLaVA-1.5, the foundational open-weight MLLM (5,491 citations) underlying much of the vision-instruction-tuning lineage baselines MMMU-Pro evaluates against.

title: LLaVA-OneVision: Easy Visual Task Transfer
authors: Bo Li, Yuanhan Zhang, Dong Guo, Renrui Zhang, Feng Li, Hao Zhang, Kaichen Zhang, et al.
why: The strongest fully-open MLLM MMMU-Pro itself evaluates as a baseline (its 38.0%→24.0% Standard→Vision drop is one of the paper's sharpest examples).

title: MiniCPM-V: A GPT-4V Level MLLM on Your Phone
authors: Yuan Yao, Tianyu Yu, Ao Zhang, Chongyi Wang, Junbo Cui, Hongji Zhu, Tianchi Cai, et al.
why: Efficient on-device MLLM baseline evaluated directly in MMMU-Pro, notable for strong OCR accuracy despite small parameter count.

title: Eyes Wide Shut? Exploring the Visual Shortcomings of Multimodal LLMs
authors: Shengbang Tong, Zhuang Liu, Yuexiang Zhai, Yi Ma, Yann LeCun, Saining Xie
why: Same critique lineage as MMMU-Pro (co-authored by MMMU-Pro's own Shengbang Tong) — diagnoses CLIP-based visual shortcomings behind why MLLMs fail to 'really see'.

title: MathVerse: Does Your Multi-modal LLM Truly See the Diagrams in Visual Math Problems?
authors: Renrui Zhang, Dongzhi Jiang, Yichi Zhang, Haokun Lin, Ziyu Guo, Pengshuo Qiu, Aojun Zhou, Pan Lu, Kai-Wei Chang, Peng Gao, Hongsheng Li
why: Same-vein benchmark critique to MMMU-Pro — strips text from math-diagram questions to test whether MLLMs truly read the figure rather than the problem statement.

title: RWKV: Reinventing RNNs for the Transformer Era
authors: Bo Peng, Eric Alcaide, Quentin Anthony, Alon Albalak, Samuel Arcadinho, Stella Biderman, et al.
why: One of RULER's own two non-Transformer baselines, evaluated directly in the paper's architecture-comparison ablation.

title: Retentive Network: A Successor to Transformer for Large Language Models
authors: Yutao Sun, Li Dong, Shaohan Huang, Shuming Ma, Yuqing Xia, Jilong Xue, Jianyong Wang, Furu Wei
why: Efficient long-context Transformer successor cited in RULER's related work on architectures for context scaling.

title: Hungry Hungry Hippos: Towards Language Modeling with State Space Models
authors: Tri Dao, Daniel Y. Fu, Khaled Kamal Saab, Armin W. Thomas, Atri Rudra, Christopher Ré
why: State-space-model architecture RULER cites as related work on efficient long-context modeling — predecessor lineage to Mamba.

title: xLSTM: Extended Long Short-Term Memory
authors: Maximilian Beck, Korbinian Poppel, Markus Spanring, Andreas Auer, Oleksandra Prudnikova, Michael Kopp, Günter Klambauer, Johannes Brandstetter, Sepp Hochreiter
why: Modern recurrent long-context architecture cited by RULER, extending the classic LSTM to compete with Transformers at scale.

title: Ring Attention with Blockwise Transformers for Near-Infinite Context
authors: Hao Liu, Matei Zaharia, Pieter Abbeel
why: Directly targets 'near-infinite context' scaling, cited in RULER's related work — complements RULER's own claimed-vs-effective-length critique.

title: DocRED: A Large-Scale Document-Level Relation Extraction Dataset
why: Document-level relation-extraction dataset that LongBench v2's structured/knowledge-graph reasoning subtask design draws on; a foundational long-document IE benchmark.

title: QMSum: A New Benchmark for Query-based Multi-domain Meeting Summarization
why: Query-based multi-domain meeting-summarization benchmark in the long-dialogue/summarization lineage LongBench v2 draws its task design from.

title: Long-Context LLMs Meet RAG: Overcoming Challenges for Long Inputs in RAG
why: Directly motivates LongBench v2's retrieval-augmented baseline experiments (Section 4.2), studying how RAG interacts with long-context LLMs.

title: pix2code: Generating Code from a Graphical User Interface Screenshot
why: Foundational vision-to-code work (screenshot to GUI code) that SWE-bench M cites as the earliest precedent for pairing visual input with code generation.

title: GPT-4V in Wonderland: Large Multimodal Models for Zero-Shot Smartphone GUI Navigation
why: Zero-shot multimodal GUI-navigation agent (smartphone screens) SWE-bench M cites among the multimodal-agent lineage its visual tooling builds on.

title: Design2Code: How Far Are We From Automating Front-End Engineering?
why: Direct sibling benchmark automating front-end engineering from screenshots — the closest existing 'visual coding' benchmark to SWE-bench M's own visual JS focus.

title: Plot2Code: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Multi-modal Large Language Models in Code Generation from Scientific Plots
why: Multimodal code-generation-from-scientific-plots benchmark cited alongside SWE-bench M's related work on multimodal code generation; extends the visual-coding-benchmark family.
